questions.q: What is middleware and how does the request pipeline work?

answer.oneLiner: In ASP.NET Core, every HTTP request passes through a pipeline of middleware components, one after another, in the exact order you registered them. Each component can inspect or modify the request, then either pass it along to the next one by calling next(), or stop right there and send a response itself. A classic example is registering exception handling at the very top so it wraps everything else — if any middleware below it throws, the exception handler catches it and returns a clean error response.
keyPoints: You build the pipeline in Program.cs using app.Use calls, and the order you write them is the exact order requests flow through them — getting it wrong causes subtle bugs.
Each middleware receives the full HttpContext — everything about the request and response — plus a next delegate it can call to hand off to the next component.
A middleware can short-circuit the pipeline by writing a response without calling next, which is exactly what authentication middleware does when a token is invalid.
The pipeline is bidirectional: any code before the await next() call runs on the way in toward the endpoint, and any code after it runs on the way back out as the response travels back to the client.
answer.detail: Think of it as an onion: the request passes inward, hits the endpoint, then the response travels back out through the same middleware in reverse.

code.note: Anything before next() runs inbound; anything after runs outbound.

redFlags: Thinking middleware runs one direction, or that order doesn't matter

dialogue.text: Can you explain what middleware is in ASP.NET Core?
Middleware are components chained into a pipeline. Each one receives the request, can do work, then calls `next` to pass control on — and it can also touch the response on the way back out.
You said "on the way back out" — what do you mean?
The pipeline is bidirectional, like an onion. Code before `await next()` runs inbound toward the endpoint; code after it runs outbound as the response unwinds back through each middleware in reverse.
So what happens if a middleware never calls `next`?
Then it short-circuits the pipeline — nothing downstream runs. That's how auth rejections or cache hits write a response early and stop the chain.

followUpsQA.a: Each component can short-circuit or transform the request before later ones run, so order changes behavior — e.g. auth must precede authorization.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if middleware doesn't call next()?
followUpsQA.a: It short-circuits: the response is written and nothing further in the pipeline executes.

followUpsQA.q: Where do you build the pipeline?
followUpsQA.a: In `Program.cs` using `app.Use...` calls; registration order is execution order.
An `HttpContext` and a `next` delegate to invoke the rest of the pipeline.

questions.q: What is the difference between app.Use, app.Run, and app.Map?

answer.oneLiner: These three methods are how you shape the middleware pipeline in ASP.NET Core. app.Use adds a middleware that does some work and then hands the request to the next component. app.Run adds a terminal endpoint that always ends the pipeline — it never calls next, so anything registered after it is dead code. app.Map branches the pipeline so requests matching a path prefix go down a separate mini-pipeline instead of continuing the main one.
keyPoints: app.Use is the standard way to add middleware that participates in the pipeline and passes control onward by calling next.
app.Run registers a terminal handler that never calls next, so it always ends the pipeline right there — if you put it first, nothing else ever runs.
app.Map splits off a branch of the pipeline for a specific path prefix, and that branch runs its own independent set of middleware that does not rejoin the main pipeline.
MapWhen lets you branch not just on path but on any condition about the request, like a header value or query string parameter.

code.note: Requests to /admin take this branch and terminate there.

dialogue.text: What's the difference between app.Use, app.Run, and app.Map?
`Use` adds middleware that can call `next` and pass control onward. `Run` is terminal — it ends the pipeline and never calls `next`. `Map` branches the pipeline based on the request path.
When would you reach for `Map`?
When I want a separate sub-pipeline for a path prefix — like everything under `/admin` getting its own middleware chain that terminates there.
If you put `app.Run` first in your pipeline, what happens?
Everything registered after it is dead code — `Run` is terminal, so it short-circuits the whole pipeline and nothing downstream ever executes.

followUpsQA.q: Is Run the only way to terminate?
followUpsQA.a: No — any `Use` middleware that doesn't call `next` also short-circuits; `Run` just makes the intent explicit.

followUpsQA.q: Can Map branch on more than a path?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — `MapWhen` branches on any predicate over the `HttpContext`, not just the path.

followUpsQA.q: Does the Map branch rejoin the main pipeline?
followUpsQA.a: No — a matched branch runs its own pipeline and doesn't fall back to the main one.

followUpsQA.q: Why does order still matter with these?
followUpsQA.a: Because `Use`/`Run`/`Map` are all registered in sequence and execute top-down.

questions.q: How does Kestrel work?

answer.oneLiner: Kestrel is the built-in, cross-platform web server that ships with ASP.NET Core — it listens on a port, accepts HTTP connections, and hands each incoming request to your middleware pipeline. It runs on Windows, Linux, and macOS with no extra installation. In production you often put a reverse proxy like Nginx or IIS in front of Kestrel to handle TLS termination, load balancing, and slow-client protection, though Kestrel can also face the internet directly if you prefer.
keyPoints: Kestrel is the default web server for ASP.NET Core and runs on every major operating system without any separate installation.
In most production setups, Kestrel sits behind a reverse proxy like IIS, Nginx, or a cloud load balancer that handles TLS, caching, and security hardening.
The reverse proxy handles concerns like TLS offload, request buffering, and slow-client protection so you do not have to bake those into your app.
Kestrel is capable of serving traffic directly to the internet, which is common in containerised deployments behind a cloud load balancer.

dialogue.text: How does Kestrel work?
Kestrel is ASP.NET Core's built-in, cross-platform web server. It listens for HTTP requests on a socket and hands each one to the middleware pipeline.
Would you expose Kestrel directly to the internet?
You can — it's fast and edge-capable — but commonly it sits behind a reverse proxy like IIS or Nginx for TLS termination, load balancing, and extra security hardening.
What does the reverse proxy actually buy you there?
Things like TLS offload, request buffering, slow-client protection, and centralized load balancing — concerns you'd rather not bake into every app instance.

followUpsQA.q: Why put a reverse proxy in front of Kestrel?
followUpsQA.a: For TLS termination, load balancing, buffering, and security — offloading cross-cutting infra concerns.
Yes — it runs on Windows, Linux, and macOS.

followUpsQA.q: What does Kestrel hand the request to?
followUpsQA.a: The middleware pipeline configured in `Program.cs`.

followUpsQA.q: Can Kestrel be used without a proxy?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — it's capable of being edge-facing directly, e.g. in containers behind a cloud load balancer.

questions.q: What is the role of Program.cs in ASP.NET Core (minimal hosting model)?

answer.oneLiner: Program.cs is the single entry point for your ASP.NET Core application — it replaced the old Startup.cs in .NET 6 and merges everything into one file. The first half, before builder.Build(), is where you register services with the dependency injection container. The second half, after builder.Build(), is where you configure the middleware pipeline with app.Use calls and finally start the server with app.Run().
keyPoints: Calling WebApplication.CreateBuilder sets up the host with default configuration, logging, and the DI container all in one step.
Everything you write before builder.Build() is service registration — you are telling the container what to create and how long to keep it alive.
Calling builder.Build() locks in your service registrations and returns the WebApplication object you use to configure the pipeline.
Everything after builder.Build() is the middleware pipeline — you layer on app.UseRouting, app.UseAuthentication, app.MapControllers, and finally app.Run() to start the server.
answer.detail: Since .NET 6 the minimal hosting model merged the old `Startup.cs` into one file. Conceptually it's still two halves: register services, then configure the pipeline.

dialogue.text: What's the role of Program.cs in the minimal hosting model?
It's the app's entry point. It builds the host, registers services into DI, builds the `WebApplication`, configures the middleware pipeline, then runs it.
How is that different from the old Startup.cs?

Since .NET 6 the minimal hosting model merged `Startup.cs` into one file. Conceptually it's still two halves — register services, then configure the pipeline — just no separate `ConfigureServices`/`Configure` methods.
Where's the dividing line in that one file?
Everything before `builder.Build()` is service registration; everything after, the `app.Use...` calls, is the pipeline. You can't add services after Build().

followUpsQA.a: The host with default config, logging, and the DI container set up.
The `WebApplication`, on which you configure the pipeline and call `Run()`.

followUpsQA.q: Can you register services after Build()?
followUpsQA.a: No — the service collection is locked once the provider is built.
`builder.Services.AddControllers()` before Build, then `app.MapControllers()` after.

questions.q: How do you write custom middleware?

answer.oneLiner: You have two options for writing custom middleware: a quick inline lambda using app.Use, or a full class that follows the convention of taking a RequestDelegate in the constructor and exposing a public InvokeAsync method. The class approach is preferred for anything reusable or testable, and you register it with app.UseMiddleware. One important gotcha is that middleware classes are effectively singletons, so you cannot inject scoped services like DbContext in the constructor — instead, add them as parameters to InvokeAsync and the framework resolves them per request.
keyPoints: An inline app.Use lambda is fine for quick, one-off middleware logic, but for anything you want to reuse or test in isolation, a dedicated class is much cleaner.

The convention-based class takes RequestDelegate as a constructor parameter — that delegate represents the rest of the pipeline — and exposes a public Task InvokeAsync method that receives the HttpContext.
Because the middleware class is instantiated once at startup and reused for every request, it behaves like a singleton, so you must not inject scoped or transient services into its constructor.
To use a scoped service like DbContext, add it as a parameter to InvokeAsync instead of the constructor — the framework will resolve a fresh instance per request that way.

code.note: The middleware is a singleton — inject scoped services via the InvokeAsync parameters, not the constructor.

redFlags: Injecting a scoped/transient service into the middleware constructor

dialogue.text: How do you write custom middleware?
Two ways: an inline `app.Use` lambda for something quick, or a convention-based class with an `InvokeAsync(HttpContext)` method that I register with `UseMiddleware<T>`.
In that class, how do you get a scoped service like a DbContext?
Not through the constructor — the middleware is effectively a singleton, so a scoped service injected there would be captured. I add it as a parameter to `InvokeAsync`, which resolves per request.
What goes wrong if you inject the DbContext in the constructor anyway?
It becomes a captive dependency — one DbContext reused across every request, causing stale data and concurrency bugs since it isn't thread-safe.

followUpsQA.q: Why is middleware effectively a singleton?
followUpsQA.a: It's constructed once at startup and reused for every request.

followUpsQA.q: How do you inject scoped services into middleware?
followUpsQA.a: As parameters on `InvokeAsync`, which the framework resolves per request.
A public `Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext ctx)` plus a constructor taking `RequestDelegate next`.

followUpsQA.q: When use a lambda vs a class?
followUpsQA.a: Lambda for small, one-off logic; a class for reusable, testable middleware.

questions.q: Why does middleware ordering matter? Give an example.

answer.oneLiner: Middleware order matters because each component runs before the ones below it, and any middleware can short-circuit the pipeline and prevent the rest from running at all. The most common mistake is getting authentication and authorization backwards — if you run authorization before authentication, there is no user identity to check permissions against, so all requests get rejected even with a valid token. Exception handling middleware should always be registered first so it acts as an outer wrapper that can catch errors thrown anywhere downstream.
keyPoints: Authentication must come before authorization in the pipeline — you have to establish who the user is before you can decide what they are allowed to do.
Exception handling middleware belongs at the very top of the pipeline so it wraps everything else and can catch errors from any component below it.
Routing middleware should be registered before any middleware that needs to know which endpoint was matched, such as CORS and authorization middleware.
CORS middleware should come after routing but before authorization, so the framework knows the matched endpoint when evaluating the CORS policy.

dialogue.text: Why does middleware ordering matter? Give an example.

Because each middleware can short-circuit or transform the request before later ones run. The classic example is authentication must come before authorization — you have to know *who* the user is before deciding *what* they can do.
What if you flip authentication and authorization?
Authorization runs against an empty identity, so authenticated users get rejected with 401/403 even with a valid token — the `ClaimsPrincipal` hasn't been built yet.
Where should exception handling sit, and why?
Near the very top, so it wraps everything downstream. If it's lower, exceptions thrown by earlier middleware escape it and you lose the consistent error response.

followUpsQA.a: `UseAuthentication()` then `UseAuthorization()` — identity before policy.
Before endpoint-aware middleware like CORS and authorization so they know the matched endpoint.
So it wraps the whole pipeline and catches exceptions from everything downstream.
After `UseRouting`, before `UseAuthorization`.

questions.q: What does it mean to short-circuit the pipeline?

answer.oneLiner: Short-circuiting means a middleware writes a response and deliberately skips calling next(), so nothing further down the pipeline runs at all. Authentication middleware does this when a token is missing or invalid — it writes a 401 response and stops, so there is no point letting the request reach the controller. It is important to understand that middleware which already ran on the way in still gets its outbound code executed on the way back out; only components that never ran are skipped.
keyPoints: A middleware short-circuits by writing to the response and not calling next(), which causes the request to turn around right there without reaching any downstream component.
Common real-world uses for short-circuiting are auth failures returning 401, cache hits returning a stored response, and rate limiting returning 429.
app.Run is a convenient way to define a middleware that always short-circuits by design, since it has no next to call.
The outbound code of any middleware that already ran on the way in still executes on the way back out — only middleware that never started is skipped.

dialogue.text: What does it mean to short-circuit the pipeline?
It means a middleware writes a response and doesn't call `next`, so nothing further downstream runs. The request turns around right there.
Give me a real case where you'd want that.
Auth rejection — if the token's invalid, write a 401 and stop. No point running the rest of the pipeline or the endpoint. Same for a cache hit or a rate-limit rejection.
Does short-circuiting skip the outbound code of earlier middleware?
No — middleware that already ran inbound still gets its outbound code on the way back out. Only the components downstream of the short-circuit are skipped.

followUpsQA.a: By writing to the response and not calling `next`.

followUpsQA.q: What's a terminal way to short-circuit?
followUpsQA.a: `app.Run`, which never calls `next` by design.
Auth failures, cache hits, and rate limiting.

followUpsQA.q: Does earlier middleware's outbound code still run?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — only downstream components are skipped; the unwinding still happens for those already entered.

questions.q: Explain the three service lifetimes: Singleton, Scoped, and Transient.

answer.oneLiner: ASP.NET Core's built-in dependency injection supports three service lifetimes, and picking the wrong one is one of the most common sources of subtle bugs. Singleton means one instance is created for the entire application lifetime and shared by everyone. Scoped means one instance per HTTP request, which is the right choice for things like DbContext that represent a unit of work. Transient means a brand-new instance every single time the service is requested from the container.
keyPoints: A singleton service is created once when the app starts and shared across all requests and all threads, so it must be thread-safe and must not hold any per-request state.
A scoped service lives for the duration of one HTTP request and is disposed when the request ends, which is why DbContext is registered as scoped by default.
A transient service gets a new instance every time it is resolved from the container, which makes it ideal for lightweight, stateless helpers that have no shared state.
The most dangerous mistake is registering DbContext as a singleton, because a single context shared across concurrent requests is not thread-safe and will corrupt change tracking.
answer.detail: In a web app the 'scope' is the HTTP request. Get the lifetime wrong and you get bugs: a singleton holding request data leaks across users; a transient `DbContext` breaks change tracking.
redFlags: Registering DbContext as singleton
Saying scoped means 'per class'

dialogue.text: Explain the three service lifetimes.
Singleton is one instance for the whole app. Scoped is one per HTTP request. Transient is a brand-new instance every time it's requested.
Which lifetime does DbContext use, and why?
Scoped — one per request. It maps to the unit of work for that request, and it's not thread-safe, so sharing it as a singleton across concurrent requests would corrupt change tracking.
What breaks if you register a singleton that holds per-request data?
It leaks across users — one request's data bleeds into another's, since the same instance is shared. That's why a singleton must be stateless or thread-safe.

followUpsQA.q: What lifetime is DbContext and why?
followUpsQA.a: Scoped — it's the per-request unit of work and isn't thread-safe.
A singleton capturing a shorter-lived service, extending its life to the singleton's.

followUpsQA.q: What must a singleton service be?
followUpsQA.a: Stateless or thread-safe, since it's shared across all requests.

followUpsQA.q: Why not register DbContext as transient?
followUpsQA.a: It breaks change tracking and unit-of-work — each repository would get its own context.

questions.q: Why use dependency injection?

answer.oneLiner: Dependency injection is the practice of giving a class its dependencies from the outside rather than having the class create them itself. The big benefit is that your classes depend on abstractions like interfaces rather than concrete implementations, which means you can swap in a mock for testing without changing any production code. ASP.NET Core has DI built in, and the container manages the entire lifecycle of your services — creating them, injecting them, and disposing them when they are no longer needed.
keyPoints: When a class receives its dependencies through its constructor rather than creating them internally, you can easily substitute a test double or alternative implementation without touching the class itself.
This aligns with the Dependency Inversion Principle from SOLID — high-level classes should depend on abstractions, not on concrete low-level details.
The DI container handles creation and disposal automatically based on each service's registered lifetime, so you never have to manually new up or dispose your services in application code.
The practical payoff in testing is huge — you just pass a mock or stub into the constructor in your unit test, so tests run without a real database, HTTP client, or any external dependency.

dialogue.text: Why use dependency injection?
It hands creation of dependencies to a container, giving loose coupling, easy testability — you can swap in mocks — and centralized lifetime management.
How does it help with testing specifically?
Because classes depend on abstractions, in a unit test I just pass a mock implementation into the constructor. No need for the real database or HTTP client.
Isn't depending on interfaces just extra ceremony?
It's the 'D' in SOLID — depend on abstractions. The payoff is swapping implementations without touching consumers, plus the container handling creation and disposal for you.

followUpsQA.q: What SOLID principle does DI embody?
followUpsQA.a: Dependency Inversion — depend on abstractions, not concretions.
The DI container, according to each service's lifetime.
You inject mock implementations into the constructor instead of real dependencies.
Constructor injection — explicit, required, immutable dependencies.

questions.q: What is the captive dependency problem (Scoped vs Singleton)?

answer.oneLiner: The captive dependency problem happens when a singleton service holds a reference to a shorter-lived scoped service, effectively trapping the scoped service and making it behave like a singleton. A typical example is a singleton background service that directly injects a scoped DbContext — that one DbContext gets reused across every request forever, which is not thread-safe and causes stale data and concurrency bugs. The fix is to inject IServiceScopeFactory instead and manually create a scope each time you need to do work, then dispose it when you're done.
keyPoints: If a singleton captures a scoped service in its constructor, the scoped service is never disposed between requests and effectively lives as long as the singleton.
A scoped DbContext held by a singleton is particularly dangerous because it is not thread-safe and accumulates stale change-tracking state across requests.
The ASP.NET Core container will detect some of these cases and throw a validation exception at startup in the development environment, but it does not catch all of them.
The correct fix is to inject IServiceScopeFactory into the singleton, then create and dispose a fresh scope each time you need access to the scoped service.

code.note: Create a fresh scope to safely resolve a scoped service from a singleton.

dialogue.text: What is the captive dependency problem?

It's when a singleton depends on a scoped service. The short-lived service gets captured and lives as long as the singleton — effectively becoming a singleton itself, which causes stale data and threading bugs.
Give a concrete example.
A singleton service injecting a scoped DbContext. That one DbContext gets reused across every request forever, so you get stale change-tracking and concurrency errors under load.
How do you fix it if the singleton genuinely needs that service?
Inject `IServiceScopeFactory`, create a fresh scope when you need the work done, and resolve the scoped service from that scope — then dispose the scope.

followUpsQA.a: In dev with scope validation on, it throws at startup for some cases — but not all.
Inject `IServiceScopeFactory` and create a scope to resolve the scoped service.

followUpsQA.q: Why is a captured DbContext dangerous?
followUpsQA.a: It's shared across concurrent requests and isn't thread-safe — stale data and corruption.

followUpsQA.q: Where does this commonly bite you?
followUpsQA.a: Background services, which are singletons needing a scoped DbContext.

questions.q: How do you register and resolve services in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: You register services in the builder.Services collection before calling builder.Build(), using methods like AddSingleton, AddScoped, or AddTransient and passing the interface and implementation types. Once the app is running, the idiomatic way to get a service is through constructor injection — you declare what you need in the constructor and the framework provides it automatically. You can also call GetRequiredService on the IServiceProvider directly when you need to resolve something outside of a constructor, but doing that inside business logic is considered an anti-pattern.
keyPoints: Service registration happens in Program.cs before builder.Build(), using builder.Services.AddScoped, AddSingleton, or AddTransient with the interface and concrete type.
Constructor injection is the preferred and idiomatic way to receive dependencies — you declare them as constructor parameters and the container resolves them automatically.
GetRequiredService throws an exception if the service is not registered, which is what you want because misconfiguration should fail loudly rather than silently return null.

Pulling services from the container directly inside your business logic is called the service locator pattern and is considered an anti-pattern because it hides dependencies and makes code harder to test.

dialogue.text: How do you register and resolve services?
Register in `builder.Services` with `Add{Lifetime}<TInterface, TImpl>()`. Resolve them with constructor injection, or explicitly via `GetRequiredService<T>()` when you don't have a constructor.
What's the difference between GetRequiredService and GetService?
`GetRequiredService<T>` throws if the service isn't registered; `GetService<T>` returns null. I prefer the throwing one so misconfiguration fails loudly at resolution time.
When is calling GetService directly a code smell?
When you do it inside business logic to pull dependencies — that's the service locator anti-pattern. It hides dependencies. Constructor injection should be the default.

followUpsQA.a: Constructor injection.
Required throws when missing; the other returns null.
Resolving dependencies manually from the container inside code, hiding them from the constructor.
In a singleton creating a scope to use scoped services.

questions.q: What is constructor injection and why is it preferred?

answer.oneLiner: Constructor injection means a class declares all of its dependencies as constructor parameters, so it is impossible to create the class without supplying everything it needs. This makes dependencies completely visible and explicit — anyone reading the class signature immediately knows what it depends on. It also makes testing straightforward because you just pass mock implementations into the constructor in your tests, with no need for a real database or HTTP client.
keyPoints: Declaring dependencies as constructor parameters makes them required and visible, so the class cannot be instantiated in an incomplete or invalid state.
Constructor injection is easy to test because you simply pass mocks or stubs into the constructor in your unit tests without needing any real infrastructure.
If a constructor is growing too large with many dependencies, that is usually a signal the class is doing too much and violating the Single Responsibility Principle — time to split it up.
Unlike property injection, constructor dependencies are typically stored in readonly fields, so they are immutable after the object is created.

dialogue.text: What is constructor injection and why is it preferred?
Dependencies are declared as constructor parameters. That makes them explicit, required, and immutable — the class simply can't be constructed in an invalid state without its dependencies.
How does that compare to property or method injection?
Property injection makes dependencies optional and mutable, which hides them and allows half-built objects. Constructor injection forces them up front, which is clearer and safer.
If a constructor has eight dependencies, what does that tell you?
It's a code smell — the class is probably doing too much, violating SRP. The fat constructor is the signal to split the responsibilities.

followUpsQA.q: Why is it easy to test?
followUpsQA.a: You just pass mocks into the constructor.

followUpsQA.q: What does a fat constructor signal?
followUpsQA.a: An SRP violation — the class has too many responsibilities.
No — typically assigned to readonly fields, so they're immutable.

followUpsQA.q: What's the downside of property injection?
followUpsQA.a: Dependencies become optional and hidden, allowing invalid object states.

questions.q: What are keyed services in .NET 8?

answer.oneLiner: Keyed services, introduced in .NET 8, let you register multiple implementations of the same interface under different string keys and then ask for a specific one by name. Before this feature, if you had two different INotifier implementations — say one for email and one for SMS — you had to inject the full collection and pick the right one yourself, or use a factory. Now you just tag each registration with a key and use the FromKeyedServices attribute in the constructor to say exactly which one you want.
keyPoints: Keyed services allow multiple implementations of the same interface to coexist in the DI container, each identified by a unique string key.
Before .NET 8, you would typically inject IEnumerable of the interface and filter by type yourself, or write a factory wrapper — both are awkward compared to keyed services.
You register a keyed service using AddKeyedScoped, AddKeyedSingleton, or AddKeyedTransient and pass the key as a string argument.
To consume a keyed service in a constructor, you decorate the parameter with the FromKeyedServices attribute and specify the key string you want resolved.

dialogue.text: What are keyed services in .NET 8?
They let you register multiple implementations of the same interface under different string keys, then resolve a specific one with `[FromKeyedServices("key")]`.
What problem did that solve?

Before .NET 8, if you had, say, an email and an SMS `INotifier`, you'd inject `IEnumerable<INotifier>` and pick by type, or use a factory. Keyed services let you ask for exactly the one you want by name.
How do you register and consume a keyed service?
`AddKeyedScoped<INotifier, SmsNotifier>("sms")` to register, then a constructor parameter `[FromKeyedServices("sms")] INotifier notifier` to consume it.

followUpsQA.q: How did people do this before?
followUpsQA.a: Injecting `IEnumerable<T>` and filtering, or a factory pattern.

followUpsQA.q: How do you resolve a keyed service?
followUpsQA.a: With the `[FromKeyedServices("key")]` attribute on the constructor parameter.

followUpsQA.q: Do keyed services support all lifetimes?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — there are keyed Singleton, Scoped, and Transient variants.

questions.q: What is the DI container in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: The DI container in ASP.NET Core is the built-in IoC container, exposed through the IServiceProvider interface. It holds all your service registrations and is responsible for creating service instances, injecting their dependencies, and disposing them when their lifetime ends. For most applications the built-in container is perfectly sufficient, but if you need advanced features like decorator support, property injection, or convention-based scanning, you can swap it out for a third-party container like Autofac.
keyPoints: The container is represented by the IServiceProvider interface, which is the single object you call when you need to resolve a service outside of constructor injection.
Everything you register in builder.Services before calling Build() is stored in the container, and it becomes immutable once the provider is built.
The container manages the entire lifecycle of services according to their registered lifetime — it creates them on demand, injects their dependencies recursively, and disposes them at the right time.

The built-in container is intentionally minimal; you can replace it with a third-party container like Autofac when you need features it does not support out of the box, such as decorators or assembly scanning.

dialogue.text: What is the DI container in ASP.NET Core?
It's the built-in IoC container, exposed as `IServiceProvider`. It holds your registrations and creates, injects, and disposes service instances according to their lifetimes.
Can you replace it with something like Autofac?
Yes — the built-in container is intentionally minimal. You can plug in a third-party container like Autofac when you need advanced features such as decorators or assembly scanning.
When would the built-in container not be enough?
When you need things it doesn't support out of the box — property injection, decorators, convention-based registration. For most apps it's perfectly sufficient.

followUpsQA.a: `IServiceProvider`.
Yes — plug in Autofac or another container via the host builder.
Creation, injection, and disposal of services per their lifetime.
`builder.Services` in `Program.cs` before Build().

questions.q: What makes an API RESTful?

answer.oneLiner: A RESTful API follows a set of conventions built on top of HTTP that make it predictable and easy to work with. The key ideas are that every resource is identified by a URI that uses nouns rather than verbs, the HTTP method tells you what action you are performing, the server never stores session state between requests, and responses use meaningful status codes to tell the client what happened. A clean example would be GET /orders/42 to retrieve a specific order rather than something like POST /getOrder?id=42.
keyPoints: Every resource is identified by a URI using nouns, so the path describes what you are accessing rather than what action you want, because the HTTP method already carries that information.
Statelessness means each request must include everything the server needs to process it — the server does not remember previous requests, which makes horizontal scaling much easier.
The HTTP method carries the intent: GET reads without side effects, POST creates, PUT replaces entirely, PATCH updates partially, and DELETE removes.
Status codes communicate the outcome precisely — 2xx for success, 3xx for redirection, 4xx for client mistakes, and 5xx for server failures.
redFlags: Verbs in URLs
Using GET to mutate data
Returning 200 for everything

dialogue.text: What makes an API RESTful?
It's stateless, models resources with URIs, uses HTTP verbs for actions and status codes for outcomes, and returns representations like JSON.
Why does statelessness matter?
Each request is self-contained — no server-side session. That means any instance can serve any request, so you scale horizontally without sticky sessions or a shared session store.
Show me a non-RESTful URL and fix it.
`POST /getOrder?id=42` is wrong — a verb in the path and a mutating-looking GET style. RESTful is `GET /orders/42`: a noun resource, the verb expressed by the HTTP method.

followUpsQA.q: Why nouns not verbs in URIs?
followUpsQA.a: The HTTP method already carries the verb; the URI identifies the resource.
Any instance can serve any request — no sticky sessions needed.

followUpsQA.q: What status code family means client error?
followUpsQA.a: 4xx; 5xx is server error.
GET read, POST create, PUT/PATCH update, DELETE remove.

questions.q: What is the difference between PUT and PATCH?

answer.oneLiner: PUT and PATCH both update a resource but in fundamentally different ways. PUT is a full replacement — you send the entire representation of the resource, and any field you omit gets reset to its default or null. PATCH is a partial update — you send only the fields you want to change, leaving everything else untouched. PUT is also defined as idempotent, meaning you can safely repeat it and always end up with the same result, while PATCH is not inherently idempotent depending on what the operation does.
keyPoints: With PUT, you are saying the resource should now look exactly like what you sent, so any field missing from the body gets reset — it is a complete replacement, not a merge.
PATCH only touches the fields included in the request body, leaving all other fields on the resource unchanged, which is what you want for partial edits.
PUT is idempotent by definition — sending the same body twice leaves the resource in the same state as sending it once, which makes retries safe.
PATCH is not always idempotent — a PATCH that sets a name is idempotent, but a PATCH that increments a counter is not, because repeating it changes the outcome each time.

dialogue.text: What's the difference between PUT and PATCH?
PUT replaces the entire resource — a full update — and is idempotent. PATCH applies a partial update to just the fields you send.
If a PUT omits a field, what happens?
That field gets reset to its default or null, because PUT is a full replacement. You're saying 'the resource is now exactly this'. PATCH would leave omitted fields untouched.
Is PATCH idempotent?
Not inherently. A 'set name to X' PATCH is, but an 'increment counter' style PATCH isn't — repeating it changes the result each time. PUT is idempotent by definition.

followUpsQA.a: Yes — sending the same body repeatedly yields the same state.
Not necessarily — it depends on the operation.

followUpsQA.q: What happens to fields omitted from a PUT?
followUpsQA.a: They're reset, since PUT replaces the whole resource.

followUpsQA.q: What format does PATCH often use?
followUpsQA.a: A partial body or JSON Patch (RFC 6902) describing the changes.

questions.q: How do you handle exceptions globally in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: The right approach is to handle exceptions in one central place rather than scattering try-catch blocks across every controller action. In ASP.NET Core you do this with UseExceptionHandler middleware, or in .NET 8 you can implement the IExceptionHandler interface, which gives you a clean TryHandleAsync method to override. Either way, you catch unhandled exceptions, map them to appropriate HTTP status codes, and return a consistent ProblemDetails response shape that follows the RFC 7807 standard.
keyPoints: Centralizing exception handling means you have one place that handles formatting, logging, and mapping exception types to HTTP status codes — instead of duplicating that logic in every controller.
UseExceptionHandler is the traditional middleware approach, while implementing IExceptionHandler in .NET 8 gives you a more structured and testable abstraction.
The standard response format for errors in a web API is ProblemDetails from RFC 7807, which includes fields like title, status, detail, and type.

You map specific exception types to the right status code in your handler — for example a domain NotFoundException becomes a 404, a validation exception becomes a 400, and anything unexpected becomes a 500.

dialogue.text: How do you handle exceptions globally?
Use a global mechanism — `UseExceptionHandler`, custom middleware, or `IExceptionHandler` in .NET 8 — to catch unhandled exceptions and return a consistent `ProblemDetails` response.
Why not just try/catch in each controller?
That scatters error formatting and logging everywhere and is easy to forget. A global handler centralizes it: one place to format errors, log, and map known exceptions to status codes.
How do you turn a domain NotFoundException into a 404?
In the handler I inspect the exception type and map it — `NotFoundException` to 404, validation to 400, else 500 — setting the status and writing a `ProblemDetails` body accordingly.

followUpsQA.q: What response shape should you return?
followUpsQA.a: RFC 7807 `ProblemDetails`.
Implement `IExceptionHandler` and register it with `UseExceptionHandler`.
Consistent formatting and logging in one place instead of scattered handlers.

followUpsQA.q: Should you leak stack traces in prod?
followUpsQA.a: No — return a generic message; log details server-side.

questions.q: What is the difference between Authentication and Authorization?

answer.oneLiner: Authentication and authorization are two separate steps that often get confused. Authentication answers the question of who you are — it validates your credentials or token and establishes your identity. Authorization comes after and answers the question of what you are allowed to do — it checks your roles, claims, or policies to decide whether to permit the action. In ASP.NET Core the order matters too: UseAuthentication must come before UseAuthorization in the middleware pipeline.
keyPoints: Authentication is the process of verifying identity — validating a JWT signature, checking a cookie, or confirming credentials — and it produces a ClaimsPrincipal that represents the user.
Authorization uses that ClaimsPrincipal to decide whether the authenticated user is permitted to perform the requested action based on roles, claims, or custom policies.
The pipeline order is critical: UseAuthentication must run before UseAuthorization, because authorization needs the identity that authentication establishes.
A 401 Unauthorized response means the user is not authenticated at all; a 403 Forbidden means they are authenticated but do not have permission for the specific action.
answer.analogy: AuthN is showing your passport; AuthZ is whether your boarding pass gets you into the lounge.

dialogue.text: What's the difference between authentication and authorization?
Authentication verifies *who* you are; authorization decides *what* you're allowed to do. AuthN comes first, then AuthZ.
What does authentication produce that authorization consumes?
A `ClaimsPrincipal` — the identity with its claims. AuthN builds it from a validated JWT or cookie, and AuthZ then evaluates roles, policies, and claims against it.
If a logged-in user hits an admin endpoint they're not allowed on, which status?
403 Forbidden — they're authenticated but not permitted. 401 is for when they're not authenticated at all.

followUpsQA.a: `UseAuthentication()` then `UseAuthorization()`.
The `ClaimsPrincipal` representing the user's identity and claims.
401 = not authenticated; 403 = authenticated but not authorized.

followUpsQA.q: What are the two authorization styles?
followUpsQA.a: Role-based and policy-based.

questions.q: Explain the JWT authentication flow.

answer.oneLiner: The JWT authentication flow starts when the client sends credentials, the server validates them and issues a signed token containing claims about the user, and the client then includes that token in the Authorization header of every subsequent request. The server validates the signature on each request to confirm the token has not been tampered with, without needing to store any session state. Because the payload is only signed and not encrypted, you should never put sensitive data like passwords inside a JWT — anyone who gets the token can read the claims.
keyPoints: A JWT has three Base64-encoded parts separated by dots — a header describing the algorithm, a payload containing the claims, and a signature that proves the token has not been tampered with.
The server validates the token by checking the signature using its signing key, without needing to look up session state, which is what makes JWT authentication stateless.
The payload is signed but not encrypted, so anyone who intercepts the token can read the claims — never store passwords, secrets, or sensitive personal data in the token.
A common pattern is to issue a short-lived access token paired with a longer-lived refresh token, so the user does not have to log in again when the access token expires.
redFlags: Putting sensitive data in the payload
Never expiring tokens
Storing tokens in localStorage despite XSS

dialogue.text: Walk me through the JWT authentication flow.

The client logs in, the server validates credentials and returns a signed JWT carrying claims. The client sends it as `Authorization: Bearer` on each request, and the server validates the signature and expiry — no server session needed.
Is the payload encrypted?
No — it's signed, not encrypted. Anyone can Base64-decode and read it. So you never put secrets in the payload; the signature only guarantees integrity, not confidentiality.
How do you handle a stolen token then?

Keep access tokens short-lived to limit the blast radius, and pair them with a refresh token so the client can get a new one without re-login. For instant revocation you need a server-side check or denylist.

followUpsQA.a: Header, Payload, Signature — dot-separated.
No — it's signed not encrypted; never store secrets there.
To limit damage if stolen; pair with a refresh token.
The server validates the signature without storing session state.

followUpsQA.q: How does JWT relate to OAuth?
followUpsQA.a: OAuth is the flow to get tokens; JWT is a common token format.

questions.q: What is API versioning, why is it needed, and what approaches exist?

answer.oneLiner: API versioning is how you make breaking changes to your API without forcing every existing client to update immediately. By keeping version 1 running alongside version 2, old clients continue working unchanged while new clients can adopt the improved endpoints. The most common approach is to include the version in the URL path, like api/v1/orders, because it is explicit and easy to test, but you can also version through a query string parameter or a custom request header.
keyPoints: Versioning lets you introduce breaking changes — like renaming fields or changing response shapes — without immediately breaking existing clients that depend on the old behavior.
The most common approach is putting the version in the URL path, like /api/v2/orders, because it is visible, cache-friendly, and easy to test with any HTTP tool.
Query string versioning, like ?api-version=2.0, keeps the URL path clean but is less obvious and can be easy to miss.
Header versioning, like api-version: 2.0, is the most RESTful approach but harder to discover and test since the version is hidden from the URL.

dialogue.text: What is API versioning and why is it needed?
Versioning lets you evolve an API without breaking existing clients. When you make a breaking change, old clients keep hitting v1 while new ones move to v2.
What approaches exist?
URL path like `/api/v1/orders`, query string `?api-version=1.0`, a custom header, or media-type versioning via the Accept header. URL path is the most common and most visible.
Which would you pick and what's the trade-off?

I'd default to URL path — it's explicit and cache-friendly. Header/media-type keeps URLs clean and is more 'pure' REST, but it's harder to test and discover. I'd use the `Asp.Versioning` package either way.

followUpsQA.a: URL path versioning, e.g. `/api/v1/...`.
`Asp.Versioning`.
To make breaking changes without breaking existing clients.

followUpsQA.q: What's the downside of header versioning?
followUpsQA.a: Harder to discover and test than a visible URL segment.

questions.q: What is idempotency and which HTTP methods are idempotent?

answer.oneLiner: Idempotency means you can perform an operation multiple times and always get the same end result as if you had done it once. This matters most when clients need to retry requests after a timeout or network failure. GET, PUT, DELETE, and HEAD are idempotent by definition — deleting a resource twice still leaves it deleted. POST is not idempotent, which is why retrying a POST to create an order can accidentally create duplicates.
keyPoints: An idempotent operation has the same effect whether you do it once or a hundred times, which makes it safe to retry without worrying about duplicate side effects.
GET and HEAD are naturally idempotent because they just read data without making any changes.
PUT is idempotent because sending the same complete resource representation repeatedly always results in the same final state.
DELETE is idempotent because deleting a resource that is already gone still leaves the world in the same state — the resource does not exist either way.
POST is not idempotent by default, but you can make it safe to retry by having the client include a unique idempotency key that the server uses to detect and deduplicate repeated requests.

dialogue.text: What is idempotency and which HTTP methods are idempotent?
An idempotent operation gives the same result no matter how many times you repeat it. GET, PUT, DELETE, and HEAD are idempotent; POST is not.
Why does idempotency matter in practice?
It makes retries safe. If a client times out and retries a PUT or DELETE, there's no harm. Retry a POST and you might create a duplicate order.
How would you make a POST safe to retry?
With an idempotency key — the client sends a unique key, the server records it, and a repeat with the same key returns the original result instead of creating a duplicate.

followUpsQA.a: No — it typically creates a new resource each call.
Deleting twice leaves the same end state — resource gone.

followUpsQA.q: How do you make POST idempotent?
followUpsQA.a: Use a client-supplied idempotency key the server deduplicates on.
GET and HEAD — read-only, no side effects.

questions.q: How does model binding and validation work in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: Model binding is how ASP.NET Core automatically maps incoming request data — from the URL path, query string, body, form fields, or headers — onto the parameters of your action method. Validation then runs against those bound values using data annotation attributes, and if the ApiController attribute is present, the framework automatically returns a 400 Bad Request with error details if anything fails validation. This saves you from writing repetitive parsing and validation code in every action.
keyPoints: The framework reads different parts of the request based on binding source attributes: FromRoute pulls from the URL path, FromQuery from the query string, FromBody from the JSON body, and so on.
The ApiController attribute activates automatic model validation, so the framework returns a 400 response with a ProblemDetails body before your action even runs if the model state is invalid.
Validation rules are expressed through data annotation attributes on your model class, like Required to make a field mandatory, Range to restrict numeric values, and MaxLength to cap string length.
Without the ApiController attribute you have to manually check ModelState.IsValid inside every action and return a 400 yourself, which is easy to forget.

dialogue.text: How does model binding and validation work?
Model binding maps request data — route, query, body, form, headers — onto your action parameters. With `[ApiController]`, validation runs from data annotations and auto-returns 400 on failure.
How does it know where to pull each value from?

By convention plus attributes: `[FromRoute]`, `[FromQuery]`, `[FromBody]`, `[FromForm]`, `[FromHeader]`. `[ApiController]` also infers complex types come from the body so you often don't annotate at all.
What if you forget [ApiController]?
Then automatic 400-on-invalid-ModelState doesn't kick in — you'd have to check `ModelState.IsValid` manually in every action or add a filter.

followUpsQA.a: Auto-returns 400 when `ModelState` is invalid.

followUpsQA.q: What are the binding source attributes?
followUpsQA.a: `[FromRoute]`, `[FromQuery]`, `[FromBody]`, `[FromForm]`, `[FromHeader]`.
Data annotations like `[Required]` and `[Range]`.

followUpsQA.q: How do you avoid over-posting via binding?
followUpsQA.a: Bind to a purpose-built DTO, not your EF entity.

questions.q: How do JWT and OAuth 2.0 relate for API security?

answer.oneLiner: OAuth 2.0 and JWT operate at different layers and are often confused. OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that defines the flows and protocols a client uses to obtain an access token from an authorization server. JWT, which stands for JSON Web Token, is just a format for representing that token in a compact, self-contained, signed structure. You can use OAuth without JWT — tokens can be opaque strings — but JWT is a popular choice because the resource server can validate the token locally without calling the authorization server.
keyPoints: OAuth 2.0 is a protocol that describes how a client application requests and receives an access token — it defines the flows like Authorization Code, Client Credentials, and Device Code.
JWT is just a token format — a signed JSON payload — and is commonly used as the format for OAuth access tokens, but the two concepts are independent of each other.
OpenID Connect is a layer on top of OAuth 2.0 that adds authentication, standardizing how identity information is included in an ID token that is always a JWT.
Your resource server validates the bearer JWT by checking the signature and expiry locally, without needing to call back to the authorization server on every request.
redFlags: Saying OAuth and JWT are the same thing

dialogue.text: How do JWT and OAuth 2.0 relate?
OAuth 2.0 is the authorization *framework* — it defines how a client obtains an access token. JWT is a common token *format* used to carry that token's claims. Different layers.
So is a JWT required for OAuth?
No — OAuth tokens can be opaque reference tokens too. JWT just happens to be a popular self-contained format because the resource server can validate it locally without a lookup.
Where does OpenID Connect fit?
OIDC sits on top of OAuth 2.0 to add authentication — it standardizes an ID token (a JWT) about who the user is. OAuth alone is authorization; OIDC adds the 'who'.

followUpsQA.q: Is OAuth the same as JWT?
followUpsQA.a: No — OAuth is the flow; JWT is a token format.
Authentication on top of OAuth, via a standardized ID token.

followUpsQA.q: What does your API do with the token?
followUpsQA.a: Validates the bearer JWT's signature and expiry.
Yes — they can be opaque reference tokens validated by introspection.

questions.q: What is the difference between Filters and Middleware? When do you use which?

answer.oneLiner: Middleware and filters both let you run cross-cutting code around requests, but they operate at completely different layers of the stack. Middleware runs on the raw HTTP pipeline for every single request before any MVC routing happens, so it knows nothing about controllers, actions, or model binding. Filters run later, inside the MVC action pipeline, and they have full access to the matched action, its bound parameters, and the model state — which is exactly what you need for concerns like validation, logging of action arguments, or result transformation.
keyPoints: Middleware runs on every request in the raw HTTP pipeline and has no knowledge of which controller or action will handle it — it only sees the HttpContext.
Filters run inside the MVC pipeline after routing has matched an endpoint, so they have access to the action descriptor, the bound model arguments, ModelState, and the action result.
The filter execution order within MVC is Authorization, then Resource, then Action, then Exception, then Result — each type runs at a specific stage.

Use middleware for cross-cutting HTTP concerns like logging, CORS, compression, and authentication that apply globally regardless of the framework layer; use filters when you need MVC-specific context.

dialogue.text: What's the difference between filters and middleware?
Middleware works on the raw HTTP pipeline for every request. Filters run inside the MVC pipeline with access to the action and model state. So middleware for HTTP concerns, filters for MVC concerns.
Give me a case that genuinely needs a filter, not middleware.

Anything needing the bound action arguments or `ModelState` — like a filter that validates the model and short-circuits, or one that inspects the action's parameters. Middleware can't see any of that.
And something better as middleware?
Cross-cutting HTTP concerns that apply to every request including static files — logging, CORS, response compression, global exception handling. Those don't need MVC context.

followUpsQA.q: What can filters access that middleware can't?
followUpsQA.a: The action, its bound arguments, `ModelState`, and the result.
Authorization → Resource → Action → Exception → Result.
For cross-cutting HTTP concerns like logging, CORS, and compression.

followUpsQA.q: Does middleware run for static files?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — it runs for every request; filters only when MVC routing matches.

questions.q: What are the different types of filters in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: ASP.NET Core MVC has five types of filters, each designed to run at a specific point in the action execution pipeline. Authorization filters run first and are responsible for short-circuiting requests that are not permitted, so no unnecessary work gets done. Resource filters wrap the entire model binding process. Action filters run just before and after the action method itself. Exception filters handle unhandled exceptions thrown by actions. And result filters run around the result execution, before and after the response is written.
keyPoints: Authorization filters run before anything else and should short-circuit immediately if the user is not permitted, so the framework never wastes time on model binding or executing the action.

Resource filters wrap model binding, which means they run before binding on the way in and after it on the way out — this makes them ideal for caching, where you can skip binding entirely and return a cached response.
Action filters run just before and after the action method executes, so on the way in they can inspect or modify the bound arguments, and on the way out they can inspect or transform the result.
Exception filters handle exceptions thrown by action methods, letting you convert specific exception types into structured HTTP responses in one place.
Result filters run around the execution of the action result itself, so they run after the action has returned a result but before that result is actually written to the response.

dialogue.text: What are the different types of filters?
Five: Authorization, Resource, Action, Exception, and Result filters — each runs at a specific stage of the MVC action pipeline.
Which runs first and what's it good for?
Authorization filters run first and short-circuit unauthorized requests before any other work — that's where `[Authorize]` lives. No point binding the model if the user isn't allowed in.
Where would you put response caching, and why there?
A resource filter — it wraps model binding, so it can short-circuit and return a cached response before binding even runs, saving that work entirely.

followUpsQA.a: Authorization filters.

followUpsQA.q: Where does a resource filter sit?
followUpsQA.a: Around model binding — before and after it.
The Exception filter.

followUpsQA.q: Why is a resource filter good for caching?
followUpsQA.a: It can short-circuit before model binding, skipping that work.

questions.q: What are Action Results in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: When a controller action needs to return an HTTP response, it does so through an IActionResult — an object that encapsulates both the status code and any response body. ASP.NET Core provides a set of helper methods on the ControllerBase class that create the right result for common scenarios: Ok wraps a 200, NotFound returns a 404, BadRequest a 400, CreatedAtAction a 201 with a Location header, and NoContent a 204 for successful operations with no body to return. Using these helpers rather than hardcoding status codes makes your intent clear.
keyPoints: IActionResult is an abstraction for the HTTP response — the framework calls ExecuteResultAsync on it to write the actual status code and body to the response.
The Ok helper returns a 200 response with the supplied object serialized as JSON in the body.
CreatedAtAction returns a 201 response and also sets the Location response header to the URL of the newly created resource, which is the correct REST behavior for a successful create operation.
BadRequest returns a 400, NotFound a 404, and NoContent a 204 — a successful response with no body, which is the right thing to return from a DELETE that succeeded.

ActionResult of T is a generic variant that tells the framework and Swagger tooling what the success response body type is, while still letting you return error results like NotFound or BadRequest from the same method.
redFlags: Returning 200 with an error message instead of the right 4xx/5xx

dialogue.text: What are Action Results in ASP.NET Core?

`IActionResult` represents the HTTP response an action returns, with helper methods that pair the right status code and payload — `Ok()`, `CreatedAtAction()`, `BadRequest()`, `NotFound()`, `NoContent()`.
What does CreatedAtAction give you that Ok doesn't?
A 201 status plus a `Location` header pointing at the new resource, which is the correct REST response to a successful create. `Ok` is just 200 with a body.
What would you return from a successful DELETE?
`NoContent()` — 204, success with no body. Returning 200 with an empty body works but 204 is the precise semantic.

followUpsQA.a: 200 with the supplied object as the body.
201 via `CreatedAtAction`, including a `Location` header.
204 — a successful response with no body, e.g. DELETE.
A typed variant combining a body type with action-result helpers.

questions.q: What is CORS and how do you enable it?

answer.oneLiner: CORS stands for Cross-Origin Resource Sharing and it is a browser security mechanism that prevents JavaScript code on one domain from making requests to a different domain unless the server explicitly says it is okay. The important thing to understand is that CORS is enforced entirely by the browser — a non-browser client like Postman or curl completely ignores it. In ASP.NET Core you configure CORS by defining a named policy that specifies which origins, HTTP methods, and headers are allowed, then applying it with app.UseCors.
keyPoints: CORS is a browser-only security restriction — it blocks JavaScript fetch or XMLHttpRequest calls to a different origin, but non-browser clients like Postman are completely unaffected.
You define a CORS policy in AddCors by specifying the allowed origins, methods, and headers, then apply the policy globally with app.UseCors or selectively with the EnableCors attribute.
UseCors must come after UseRouting in the pipeline and before UseAuthorization, so the framework knows the matched endpoint when evaluating the policy.

During a cross-origin request, the browser first sends a preflight OPTIONS request to check whether the server allows the operation, and the CORS middleware responds with the appropriate Access-Control headers.

dialogue.text: What is CORS and how do you enable it?
CORS is a browser security mechanism that blocks JavaScript calls to a different origin unless the server opts in via response headers. In ASP.NET Core you define a named policy and call `UseCors`.
Who actually enforces CORS — the server or the browser?
The browser. The server just advertises which origins are allowed; the browser is what blocks the JS from reading the response. A non-browser client like Postman ignores CORS entirely.
Where does UseCors go in the pipeline?
After `UseRouting` and before `UseAuthorization`. Get it out of order and either CORS headers are missing or preflight requests fail.

followUpsQA.a: The browser — not the server.
After `UseRouting`, before `UseAuthorization`.
No — it's browser-only; non-browser clients ignore it.

followUpsQA.q: What do you configure in a policy?
followUpsQA.a: Allowed origins, headers, and methods.

questions.q: What is the difference between role-based and policy-based authorization?

answer.oneLiner: Role-based and policy-based authorization are two ways to control access in ASP.NET Core. Role-based is the simpler approach — you decorate an endpoint with something like Authorize with a Roles parameter set to Admin and the framework checks whether the user's identity includes that role claim. Policy-based authorization is more flexible and expressive — you define a named policy with one or more requirements, write a handler that evaluates them against the user's claims and the context, and attach the policy name to the endpoint.
keyPoints: Role-based authorization is quick to set up and works well for simple scenarios — you specify one or more required role names and the framework checks the user's role claims.
Policy-based authorization is more powerful because a policy can combine multiple requirements — like requiring a specific claim, a minimum age, and membership in a department — all in one check.
To create a custom policy you define a class that implements IAuthorizationRequirement and a corresponding AuthorizationHandler that contains the logic for evaluating it.

Role-based authorization tends to fall apart as business rules get more complex, because you end up with dozens of fine-grained roles or messy combinations — policies scale much better in those cases.

dialogue.text: Role-based vs policy-based authorization — what's the difference?

Role-based checks a user's role, like `[Authorize(Roles="Admin")]`. Policy-based checks flexible requirements built from claims or custom logic, like `[Authorize(Policy="Over18")]` — it's far more expressive.
When does role-based start to fall apart?

When rules get compound — 'admin OR owns the resource AND in this region'. You can't express that with roles cleanly; you end up with role explosion. Policies handle it with a requirement and handler.
How do you implement a custom policy?
Define an `IAuthorizationRequirement`, write an `AuthorizationHandler` for it that evaluates the claims/context, and register the policy in `AddAuthorization`.

followUpsQA.q: What's a role in claims terms?
followUpsQA.a: A simple, coarse claim on the principal.
When rules combine multiple claims or custom logic — they scale better.
An `IAuthorizationRequirement` plus an `AuthorizationHandler`.

followUpsQA.q: What builds the claims they check?
followUpsQA.a: Authentication — the `ClaimsPrincipal`.

questions.q: Why use refresh tokens with JWTs?

answer.oneLiner: Refresh tokens exist to solve a specific tension with JWT authentication — you want access tokens to be short-lived so that if one is stolen the damage is limited, but you do not want users to have to log in again every few minutes. The solution is to pair the short-lived access token with a separate, longer-lived refresh token. When the access token expires, the client sends the refresh token to a specific endpoint and gets a new access token without the user needing to enter their credentials again.
keyPoints: Access tokens are intentionally short-lived — typically minutes to an hour — so that if one is intercepted, the attacker has only a small window before it becomes useless.
Refresh tokens are longer-lived, often lasting days or weeks, and are stored server-side so they can be revoked at any time, giving you a way to implement logout and account suspension.
When the access token expires, the client sends the refresh token to a token refresh endpoint and receives a new access token, keeping the user session alive without requiring re-login.
Refresh tokens should be stored in an httpOnly, secure cookie so that JavaScript code on the page cannot access them, which protects against XSS attacks stealing the token.

dialogue.text: Why use refresh tokens with JWTs?
Access tokens are kept short-lived to limit damage if one's stolen. A longer-lived refresh token lets the client silently get a new access token without forcing the user to log in again.
JWTs are self-validating — so how does this give you revocation?

The access token still can't be revoked mid-life, but the refresh token is stored server-side and revocable. Revoke it and the client can't mint new access tokens, so access dies within the short access-token window.
Where should the refresh token live on the client?
An httpOnly, secure cookie — so JavaScript and XSS can't read it. Never in localStorage. And rotate it on each use.

followUpsQA.a: To limit the blast radius if one is stolen.

followUpsQA.q: How do refresh tokens enable revocation?
followUpsQA.a: They're stored server-side and revocable, unlike a self-contained JWT.

followUpsQA.q: Where should the refresh token be stored?
followUpsQA.a: An httpOnly, secure cookie.
Issuing a new refresh token on each use and invalidating the old one.

questions.q: How does rate limiting work in ASP.NET Core, and what are the built-in algorithms?

answer.oneLiner: ASP.NET Core 7 added a built-in rate limiting middleware so you do not need a third-party library for basic throttling. You configure it with named policies and attach those policies to specific endpoints or controllers. It ships with four algorithms: fixed window limits requests per a fixed time block, sliding window smooths out burst traffic at window boundaries, token bucket allows controlled bursts by refilling tokens over time, and concurrency limits how many requests can be processed simultaneously.
keyPoints: Fixed window rate limiting allows up to N requests within a fixed time block, but it has a burst problem at the boundary where a client can fire double the limit across two consecutive windows.
Sliding window rate limiting divides the window into segments and tracks usage more granularly, which smooths out the edge-burst problem of fixed window.

Token bucket rate limiting works by refilling a bucket of tokens at a steady rate, and each request consumes a token — this allows short bursts up to the bucket size while still enforcing an overall average rate.
When a request exceeds the limit, the middleware returns a 429 Too Many Requests response, and you can also include a Retry-After header telling the client when to try again.

code.note: Returns 429 Too Many Requests when the limit is exceeded.

redFlags: Not knowing 429 is the rejection code
Thinking fixed window has no burst problem

dialogue.text: How does rate limiting work, and what algorithms ship in ASP.NET Core?
ASP.NET Core 7+ has a built-in rate limiter middleware with four algorithms: fixed window, sliding window, token bucket, and concurrency. You add `UseRateLimiter` and attach policies to endpoints.
What's the downside of fixed window?
Burstiness at the edges. With 100/minute, a client can fire 100 at 0:59 and another 100 at 1:00 — 200 in two seconds across the window boundary. Sliding window smooths that with sub-windows.
What status code does a rejected request get?
429 Too Many Requests. You can also send a `Retry-After` header telling the client when to try again.

followUpsQA.a: 429 Too Many Requests.
Fixed is cheap but bursty at edges; sliding smooths bursts with sub-windows.
Controlled bursts — tokens refill over time.

followUpsQA.q: How do you apply a policy?
followUpsQA.a: `UseRateLimiter()` plus `[EnableRateLimiting("name")]` on the endpoint.

questions.q: What is SignalR, when would you use it, and what transports does it use?

answer.oneLiner: SignalR is an ASP.NET Core library that makes it easy to add real-time, push-based communication to your application — the server can send messages to connected clients at any time, not just in response to a request. It automatically negotiates the best transport available, preferring WebSockets for full-duplex communication, then falling back to Server-Sent Events and finally long polling if WebSockets is not available. The programming model is built around Hubs, which are classes where you define methods that both the server and client can call on each other.
keyPoints: SignalR enables real-time server-to-client communication, which is what you need for features like live chat, stock tickers, collaborative editing, or live notifications.
The library automatically negotiates the best available transport — WebSockets is the preference, then Server-Sent Events for server-to-client only, then long polling as the universal fallback.
The Hub is the central abstraction — it is a class on the server where you define methods that clients can call, and from which you can push messages to individual clients, groups, or everyone.

When you scale out to multiple server instances, each server only knows about its own connected clients, so you need a backplane like Redis or Azure SignalR Service to broadcast messages across all instances.
redFlags: Using polling when push is needed
Not knowing the transport fallback order

dialogue.text: What is SignalR and when would you use it?

SignalR is a library for real-time, bidirectional server-to-client messaging. I'd use it for chat, live dashboards, notifications, and collaborative apps — anywhere the server needs to push, not just respond.
What transports does it use?
It prefers WebSockets, and falls back to Server-Sent Events, then long polling, if WebSockets isn't available. It negotiates the best one automatically.
You scale to three servers — what breaks and how do you fix it?
Clients connect to different servers, so a message sent from one isn't seen by clients on the others. You add a backplane — Redis or Azure SignalR — so messages fan out across all servers.

followUpsQA.a: WebSockets → Server-Sent Events → long polling.

followUpsQA.q: Why is a backplane needed when scaling out?
followUpsQA.a: To broadcast messages across servers so clients on any instance receive them.
A class exposing RPC-style methods both server and client can call.
WebSockets is full-duplex; SSE is server-to-client only.

questions.q: Should you use session-based authentication in a REST API? Why is REST ideally stateless?

answer.oneLiner: REST is designed around statelessness — every request must contain all the information the server needs to fulfill it, with no server-side session remembered between calls. This is why JWT-based authentication fits REST so well: the token carries the user's identity in each request, and any server instance can validate it independently. Session-based authentication breaks this model because session state is stored on the server, which means requests must go to the same server or you need a shared session store — both hurt scalability.
keyPoints: Statelessness means the server does not store any memory of previous requests, so each request is completely self-contained and any instance in a cluster can handle it without coordinating with others.
JWT authentication fits the REST stateless model perfectly because the token carries the user's identity and claims directly in the request, removing the need for any server-side session lookup.
Session-based authentication requires server-side state, which forces you to either use sticky sessions pinning users to one server or set up a shared distributed session store like Redis.

The trade-off with stateless tokens is that they are hard to revoke mid-life, whereas sessions can be invalidated instantly — you address this by keeping access tokens short-lived and using revocable refresh tokens.
redFlags: Saying sessions scale as well as tokens
Not knowing why statelessness matters

dialogue.text: Should you use session-based auth in a REST API?

Ideally no. REST is meant to be stateless — each request carries everything needed — so token-based auth like JWT fits better than server-side sessions, which add server state and hurt horizontal scaling.
Why exactly do sessions hurt scaling?
Session state lives on the server, so you need sticky sessions pinning a user to one instance, or a shared session store. Tokens are self-contained, so any instance can serve any request freely.
But tokens are hard to revoke — how do you handle logout?

That's the trade-off. You keep access tokens short-lived and use a revocable refresh token, or maintain a small server-side denylist for immediate revocation — reintroducing a bit of state on purpose.

followUpsQA.a: So any instance can serve any request — free horizontal scaling.

followUpsQA.q: How do you revoke a stateless JWT?
followUpsQA.a: Short lifetimes plus a revocable refresh token, or a denylist.

followUpsQA.q: When is a session still appropriate?
followUpsQA.a: For server-rendered web apps, not pure APIs.

followUpsQA.q: What do sessions require to scale?
followUpsQA.a: Sticky sessions or a shared session store.

questions.q: When do you return 404 vs 500, and what's the general 4xx vs 5xx distinction?

answer.oneLiner: HTTP status codes are organized into classes that tell you at a glance who is responsible for the problem. Codes in the 4xx range mean the client made a mistake — the request is wrong in some way. Codes in the 5xx range mean the server failed to handle an otherwise valid request. Getting this right matters because clients and monitoring systems use these codes to decide how to react — a 4xx tells the client to fix something, a 5xx tells operations there is a server problem to investigate.
keyPoints: 4xx codes indicate a client error — the problem is with the request itself, and the client needs to change something about it before retrying.
404 Not Found means the resource the client requested does not exist, which is a client-side problem because they asked for something that is not there.
400 Bad Request means the client sent a malformed or invalid request, such as missing required fields or sending the wrong data types.
5xx codes indicate a server error — the request was valid but the server failed to process it, which is a problem to investigate on the server side.
A common mistake is returning 500 when a record is not found — the right code is 404 because the resource simply does not exist, not because something went wrong on the server.
redFlags: Returning 500 for a missing record (should be 404)
Returning 200 with an error body

dialogue.text: When do you return 404 vs 500?

404 means the client asked for something that doesn't exist — the request is the problem. 500 means the server failed while processing an otherwise valid request. Broadly, 4xx is client error, 5xx is server error.
An unhandled NullReferenceException reaches the client — which code, and is that good?
It surfaces as 500. That's the right *code*, but a raw exception leaking out isn't good — a global handler should catch it and return a clean `ProblemDetails` without the stack trace.
Give me 401 vs 403.
401 is unauthenticated — no or invalid credentials. 403 is authenticated but not permitted. Don't conflate them.

followUpsQA.a: 4xx = client error, 5xx = server error.
401 unauthenticated; 403 authenticated but forbidden.

followUpsQA.q: How should a 500 reach the client?
followUpsQA.a: As a clean `ProblemDetails`, via a global handler — no stack trace.

questions.q: How do you do parallel processing in a Web API, and how do you throttle it?

answer.oneLiner: When you have multiple independent async operations that do not depend on each other, you can run them concurrently with Task.WhenAll instead of awaiting them one by one, which can dramatically reduce total elapsed time.
