However, firing an unbounded number of concurrent tasks at a database or downstream API is dangerous — you can exhaust connection pools, trigger throttling, or even take a dependency down. The safe approach is to cap the degree of parallelism using something like SemaphoreSlim to limit how many tasks run at the same time, or the newer Parallel.ForEachAsync which has a MaxDegreeOfParallelism option built in.
keyPoints: Task.WhenAll starts multiple async operations simultaneously and waits for all of them to complete, rather than waiting for each one to finish before starting the next.
Firing an unbounded number of concurrent requests to a database or external service can exhaust connection pools, overwhelm the downstream service, and cause cascading failures.
SemaphoreSlim is a lightweight mechanism to cap concurrency — you acquire a slot before starting each operation and release it when done, so no more than N operations run simultaneously.
Parallel.ForEachAsync is the cleaner modern alternative for async workloads — you set MaxDegreeOfParallelism to cap the number of concurrent iterations without manually managing a semaphore.
Parallel.For and Parallel.ForEach are designed for CPU-bound work that runs on the thread pool, and they are the wrong tool for async I/O-bound operations like database calls or HTTP requests.

code.note: Caps concurrency at 5 in-flight requests.

redFlags: Firing thousands of concurrent calls with no limit
Using Parallel.For for I/O-bound work

dialogue.text: How do you do parallel processing in a Web API and throttle it?
Run independent async work concurrently with `Task.WhenAll`, and throttle the degree of parallelism with `SemaphoreSlim` or `Parallel.ForEachAsync` so you don't overwhelm downstream systems.
Why not just fire a thousand tasks with WhenAll?
Unbounded concurrency hammers the DB or downstream API — connection-pool exhaustion, timeouts, throttling, maybe taking the dependency down. You want a cap on in-flight work.
When is Parallel.For the wrong tool?
For I/O-bound async work. `Parallel.For` is for CPU-bound work and blocks threads. For I/O you want `Parallel.ForEachAsync` or `SemaphoreSlim`-gated tasks that release the thread while awaiting.

followUpsQA.q: Why throttle instead of unbounded WhenAll?
followUpsQA.a: To avoid exhausting connections and overwhelming downstreams.
`SemaphoreSlim` or `Parallel.ForEachAsync` with `MaxDegreeOfParallelism`.
WhenAll awaits a set of tasks; ForEachAsync also caps concurrency for you.
No — it's for CPU-bound work; use async for I/O.

questions.q: What causes a deadlock in async code, and how do you prevent it?

answer.oneLiner: The classic async deadlock happens when you block synchronously on an async operation — using .Result or .Wait() — in an environment that has a single-threaded synchronization context. The async continuation needs to resume on that context thread, but you are blocking it waiting for the async operation to finish, so both are waiting on each other and nothing moves. The fix is simple in principle: go async all the way down, never block on async operations, and use ConfigureAwait(false) in library code that does not need to resume on the original context.
keyPoints: The deadlock occurs when code blocks a thread using .Result or .Wait() on a Task, and that Task's continuation needs to resume on the same thread that is blocked — creating a circular wait.

ASP.NET Core does not have a SynchronizationContext like older ASP.NET did, so the classic deadlock is less likely, but blocking on async code still wastes thread pool threads and causes starvation under load.
The rule to follow is async all the way — if a method is async, everything that calls it should also be async, and you should never block with .Result or .Wait() anywhere in the call chain.

ConfigureAwait(false) tells the awaiter not to capture the current synchronization context, so the continuation can run on any thread pool thread — this is standard practice in library code that does not need UI or request context.

code.note: Async all the way down; reserve ConfigureAwait(false) for non-context-dependent library code.

redFlags: Calling .Result/.Wait() in request handling
Not knowing async-all-the-way

dialogue.text: What causes a deadlock in async code, and how do you prevent it?

Blocking on async with `.Result` or `.Wait()` while the captured synchronization context is needed to resume the continuation. The continuation waits for the context thread, which is blocked waiting for the continuation — deadlock. Fix: go async all the way, and use `ConfigureAwait(false)` in libraries.
ASP.NET Core has no SynchronizationContext — so is blocking fine there?
It won't classically deadlock, but it's still bad — blocking ties up a thread-pool thread, and under load you get thread-pool starvation and latency spikes. Still async all the way.
Why does ConfigureAwait(false) help?
It tells the await not to capture and resume on the original context, so the continuation runs on any thread-pool thread — no contention for the captured context. It's standard in library code.

followUpsQA.a: Blocking the context thread the awaited continuation needs to resume.

followUpsQA.q: Does ASP.NET Core have a SynchronizationContext?
followUpsQA.a: No — but blocking still causes thread-pool starvation.
Async all the way; never `.Result`/`.Wait()` on async.
In library code that doesn't depend on the context.

questions.q: What is a refresh token for, and can it live on the frontend?

answer.oneLiner: A refresh token is a long-lived credential that allows a client to obtain a new short-lived access token when the current one expires, without making the user log in again. On the frontend, the refresh token must be stored somewhere the JavaScript cannot read it — which means an httpOnly cookie, not localStorage or sessionStorage. An httpOnly cookie is inaccessible to JavaScript running on the page, so even if an attacker injects a malicious script through an XSS vulnerability, they cannot steal the refresh token.
keyPoints: The whole point of a refresh token is to let you keep access tokens very short-lived without constantly interrupting the user to log back in — the refresh token silently handles getting a new access token.
Refresh tokens should be stored in an httpOnly, Secure cookie, which the browser sends automatically but which JavaScript cannot read regardless of what scripts are running on the page.
Storing a refresh token in localStorage is a serious security mistake — any JavaScript on the page, including injected malicious code, can read localStorage and steal the token.

Refresh token rotation means the server issues a new refresh token each time the old one is used and immediately invalidates the previous one, so a stolen token can only be used once before it becomes invalid.
redFlags: Storing refresh tokens in localStorage
Never expiring/rotating them

dialogue.text: What is a refresh token for, and can it live on the frontend?
It lets the client silently get a new short-lived access token without re-login. It can be on the frontend, but only in an httpOnly, secure cookie — never in JS-accessible storage.
Why httpOnly cookie over localStorage?

localStorage is readable by any JavaScript on the page, so an XSS payload can steal the token. An httpOnly cookie is invisible to JS, so XSS can't exfiltrate it — though you then need CSRF protection.
What is refresh token rotation?
Each time a refresh token is used, the server issues a new one and invalidates the old. If an old token is replayed, that signals theft and you can revoke the whole chain.

followUpsQA.a: JS can't read httpOnly cookies, so XSS can't steal the token.
They need CSRF protection.
Issuing a new refresh token on each use and invalidating the old.
To limit damage if stolen, since they're hard to revoke.

questions.q: What is the difference between IActionResult and ActionResult<T>?

answer.oneLiner: IActionResult is the generic return type for controller actions that says an HTTP response will be returned, but it does not tell the compiler or tooling anything about what the response body will look like. ActionResult of T is the typed version that closes that gap — you can return either the typed object directly, which becomes a 200 response, or any action result helper like NotFound or BadRequest from the same method. The practical benefit beyond type safety is that Swagger and other OpenAPI tooling can automatically document the 200 response schema from the type parameter.
keyPoints: IActionResult is a non-generic interface that represents any HTTP response but carries no information about the success response body type, so tooling cannot infer the schema.
ActionResult of T uses an implicit conversion operator that lets you write return product to return a 200 with the product serialized, or return NotFound to return a 404 — both from the same method.
When you use ActionResult of T, OpenAPI and Swagger tools can read the type parameter and automatically generate the correct schema for the success response in your API documentation.
Plain IActionResult is still useful when the response body varies depending on conditions and you do not want to commit to a fixed success type.

code.note: ActionResult<T> documents the success type and still allows error results.

redFlags: Thinking they behave identically for API documentation

dialogue.text: What's the difference between IActionResult and ActionResult<T>?

`IActionResult` only describes the HTTP result — status and body — with no compile-time body type. `ActionResult<T>` lets you return either a typed `T` or any action result, improving clarity and Swagger output.
How does ActionResult<T> improve Swagger?
It tells the framework the success body type, so OpenAPI documents the 200 response schema as `T`. With bare `IActionResult`, the tooling can't infer the body type, so the schema is missing.
What lets you `return product;` directly?
An implicit conversion on `ActionResult<T>` — you can `return product;` for a 200 or `return NotFound();` for a 404 from the same method, no wrapping needed.

followUpsQA.a: It exposes the success body type for OpenAPI schema generation.
When the body type varies or doesn't matter for docs.
An implicit conversion on `ActionResult<T>`.
Typed action results like `Ok()`, `NotFound()`, `BadRequest()`.

questions.q: What's the difference between an Action Filter and Middleware, which runs first, and is [Authorize] an action filter?

answer.oneLiner: Middleware and action filters both let you run code around a request, but they live at completely different layers and that affects what information they can see. Middleware runs first on the raw HTTP pipeline before MVC gets involved, so it knows nothing about which controller or action will handle the request. Action filters run much later, inside the MVC action pipeline, and they have access to the specific action that was matched, its bound parameters, and the model state. One important distinction to know for interviews: the Authorize attribute is not an action filter — it is an authorization filter, which runs even earlier than action filters, before model binding.
keyPoints: Middleware runs on the raw HTTP pipeline for every request, before routing has matched any endpoint, so it has no knowledge of controllers, actions, or model state.
Action filters run inside the MVC pipeline after routing and model binding, so they can see the specific action being invoked, its bound parameter values, and the model state.

The full execution order from outermost to innermost is: middleware, then routing, then authorization filters, then resource filters, then model binding, then action filters, then the action method itself.

The Authorize attribute creates an authorization filter, not an action filter — authorization filters run before action filters specifically so they can reject unauthorized requests before any expensive work like model binding happens.
redFlags: Calling [Authorize] an action filter
Thinking filters run before middleware

dialogue.text: Action filter vs middleware — which runs first, and is [Authorize] an action filter?

Middleware runs first, on the raw HTTP pipeline. Action filters run later inside MVC with access to the action and model state. And no — `[Authorize]` is an authorization filter, which runs even earlier than action filters.
Spell out the order from request to action.
Middleware → routing → authorization filter → resource filter → model binding → action filter → the action method. Then results unwind back out.
Why isn't [Authorize] an action filter?
Because you want to reject unauthorized requests before doing expensive work like model binding or running action filters. Authorization filters run first precisely so they can short-circuit early.

followUpsQA.a: In the authorization filter stage — before action filters.
Middleware → routing → authorization → resource → action → the action method.

followUpsQA.q: When pick middleware over a filter?
followUpsQA.a: For cross-cutting HTTP concerns that don't need MVC context.
No — only filters can; middleware runs before MVC binding.

questions.q: What is the difference between a resource filter and an action filter?

answer.oneLiner: Resource filters and action filters are both part of the MVC filter pipeline, but they run at different stages. Resource filters wrap model binding — they execute before and after the binding process — which makes them useful for short-circuiting the entire request, such as returning a cached response without binding the model at all. Action filters run after model binding is complete, so they have access to the fully populated action arguments and can inspect or modify them before the action executes.
keyPoints: A resource filter runs before model binding on the way in, which means it can short-circuit and return a response without the framework ever spending time on binding the request body or route parameters.
Because resource filters run before binding, they do not have access to the bound model arguments — they can only see the raw HttpContext and the action descriptor.

An action filter runs after model binding, so it can read and even modify the bound action arguments before the action method runs, which is useful for cross-cutting concerns like logging the incoming command.
The pipeline order is authorization filter, then resource filter, then model binding, then action filter, and finally the action method — resource filters are always outer to action filters.
redFlags: Reversing their order
Thinking both run after model binding

dialogue.text: Resource filter vs action filter — what's the difference?
Resource filters run earlier, wrapping model binding — before and after it. Action filters run after binding, around the action method, with access to the bound arguments.
Which would you use for caching, and why?
A resource filter. Because it runs before model binding, it can short-circuit and return a cached response without paying the cost of binding or executing the action at all.
Which one sees the action's bound arguments?
The action filter — it runs after binding, so it has the populated parameters. A resource filter runs around binding, so on the way in the arguments aren't bound yet.

followUpsQA.q: Why is a resource filter good for caching?
followUpsQA.a: It can short-circuit before model binding, skipping that work.
The action filter, which runs after binding.
Authorization → Resource → binding → Action.
For model-aware logic that needs the bound arguments or result.

questions.q: Minimal APIs vs Controller-based APIs — when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: Minimal APIs and controller-based APIs are two different ways to define HTTP endpoints in ASP.NET Core, and the right choice depends on the scale and complexity of your API. Minimal APIs use a very concise syntax — you call app.MapGet or app.MapPost with a handler lambda — and they have almost no ceremony, which makes them great for microservices, small APIs, and getting something running quickly. Controller-based APIs give you a more structured approach with the full MVC feature set, including filters, model binding attributes, and conventions that scale better when you have many endpoints with complex cross-cutting concerns.
keyPoints: Minimal APIs use the app.MapGet, app.MapPost, and similar methods to define routes inline with very little boilerplate, which makes them fast to write and easy to understand for small services.

Controller-based APIs organize endpoints into controller classes and give you access to the full MVC feature set — authorization filters, action filters, model binding attributes, and conventional routing.
Minimal APIs support dependency injection natively by including service types as handler parameters, so you are not giving up DI by choosing them.
Since .NET 7, minimal APIs support endpoint filters and route groups, which closes a lot of the feature gap with MVC controllers for cross-cutting concerns like validation and logging.
Controllers tend to be a better fit for large, complex APIs with many endpoints where the convention-based structure and filter pipeline keep things organized and consistent.

code.snippet:
app.MapGet("/products/{id}", async (int id, IProductService svc) =>
    await svc.Find(id) is { } p ? Results.Ok(p) : Results.NotFound());
code.note: Dependencies are injected directly as handler parameters.

redFlags: Saying minimal APIs can't do DI or validation
Claiming controllers are always better

dialogue.text: Minimal APIs vs controller-based APIs — when do you use each?

Minimal APIs are lightweight endpoint mapping with less ceremony — great for small services and microservices. Controllers give the full MVC feature set: filters, conventions, attribute model binding, which scales better for large APIs.
People say minimal APIs can't do DI or validation — true?

No. DI works — dependencies are injected as handler parameters. And since .NET 7 you have endpoint filters and route groups, so validation and cross-cutting logic are covered. The gap with controllers has narrowed a lot.
So when would minimal APIs actually not scale well?
On a large, complex domain with lots of shared cross-cutting behavior and many endpoints — the convention-based organization and filters of controllers keep that tidier than a sprawl of map calls.

followUpsQA.a: Yes — dependencies are injected as handler parameters.

followUpsQA.q: How do you group minimal API endpoints?
followUpsQA.a: With route groups (`MapGroup`) and shared endpoint filters.

followUpsQA.q: When would minimal APIs not scale?
followUpsQA.a: On large, complex domains where MVC conventions/filters keep things organized.

followUpsQA.q: What did .NET 7 add to minimal APIs?
followUpsQA.a: Endpoint filters and route groups.

questions.q: Endpoint filters vs middleware vs MVC filters — when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: There are three places you can hook cross-cutting logic into a request in ASP.NET Core, and they suit different scenarios. Middleware runs on the raw HTTP pipeline for every request before any framework routing, so it is ideal for truly global concerns like logging, CORS, and compression. MVC filters run inside the controller pipeline and have access to MVC-specific context like action arguments and model state, so they are better for concerns that apply to controller actions specifically. Endpoint filters, introduced in .NET 7, are the minimal API equivalent of action filters — they run around a specific handler and can see the handler's typed arguments.
keyPoints: Middleware applies to every request in the pipeline regardless of routing, so it is best for truly global HTTP concerns that have nothing to do with specific controllers or endpoints.
MVC action filters run inside the controller pipeline after routing and model binding, giving them access to the action descriptor, the bound arguments, model state, and the action result.
Endpoint filters are the minimal API equivalent of action filters — they are registered per-endpoint or per route group and run around the handler with access to the typed handler arguments.
You cannot use MVC filters with minimal API endpoints, and endpoint filters do not work with controller actions — they are parallel concepts in different parts of the framework.

The key difference between middleware and the filter types is that middleware runs blind to the framework's understanding of the request, while filters have rich knowledge of what action or handler is being invoked.

code.note: Endpoint filters see the handler arguments via ctx.GetArgument<T>(index).

redFlags: Thinking endpoint filters work with controllers
Saying middleware can read ModelState

dialogue.text: Endpoint filters vs middleware vs MVC filters — when each?

Middleware runs on the raw pipeline for every request with no endpoint knowledge. MVC filters run inside the controller pipeline with action and model context. Endpoint filters, added in .NET 7, bring filter-like per-endpoint logic to minimal APIs.
Why use an endpoint filter instead of middleware in a minimal API?
Because an endpoint filter can see the handler's arguments via `ctx.GetArgument<T>`, and it's scoped to specific endpoints or a group — middleware is global and blind to the handler's parameters.
Can you use MVC filters with minimal APIs?
No — MVC filters belong to controllers. The minimal-API equivalent is the endpoint filter; they're not interchangeable.

followUpsQA.q: Why endpoint filter over middleware in minimal APIs?
followUpsQA.a: It can read handler arguments and is scoped per-endpoint.

followUpsQA.q: Do MVC filters work with minimal APIs?
followUpsQA.a: No — use endpoint filters instead.
No — it's MVC-unaware.

followUpsQA.q: How do you scope an endpoint filter?
followUpsQA.a: Per-endpoint with `AddEndpointFilter`, or per-group on a route group.

questions.q: How do you do centralised exception handling with IExceptionHandler in .NET 8?

answer.oneLiner: In .NET 8, the cleanest way to handle exceptions globally is to implement the IExceptionHandler interface, which gives you a focused TryHandleAsync method where you receive the exception and decide how to respond. You register your handler with AddExceptionHandler and activate it with UseExceptionHandler middleware, and you can also register AddProblemDetails for a built-in fallback that generates RFC 7807 formatted error responses. You can chain multiple handlers — each one gets a chance to handle the exception, and the first one that returns true wins.
keyPoints: IExceptionHandler has a single method, TryHandleAsync, that receives the HttpContext and the unhandled exception, and you return true if you handled it or false to let the next handler try.
You should return a ProblemDetails response body following the RFC 7807 standard, which gives clients a consistent error format with fields like title, status, detail, and type.
Registering AddProblemDetails gives you a fallback error response for exceptions that none of your handlers claim, so you always return a structured error rather than an empty response.

You can register multiple IExceptionHandler implementations and they are called in registration order — the first one that handles the exception stops the chain, which lets you have specialized handlers for specific exception types.

code.note: Return true once handled so later handlers/middleware are skipped.

redFlags: Try/catch in every controller instead of a global handler
Leaking stack traces to clients in prod

dialogue.text: How do you do centralized exception handling with IExceptionHandler in .NET 8?
Implement `IExceptionHandler`, register it with `AddExceptionHandler`, and pair it with `UseExceptionHandler` to turn unhandled exceptions into consistent RFC 7807 `ProblemDetails` responses.
Why is IExceptionHandler cleaner than custom middleware?

It's a focused abstraction — just a `TryHandleAsync` method — it integrates with the built-in `UseExceptionHandler` infrastructure and `AddProblemDetails`, and you can chain multiple handlers, first to return true wins.
How do you map specific exception types to status codes?
Inside `TryHandleAsync` I switch on the exception type — a domain `NotFoundException` to 404, validation to 400 — set the status and write the matching `ProblemDetails`; return true once handled.

followUpsQA.a: A standard JSON error format with title, status, detail, and type fields.

followUpsQA.q: How do you map exception types to status codes?
followUpsQA.a: Switch on the type inside `TryHandleAsync` and set the status accordingly.
Signals the exception is handled so later handlers/middleware are skipped.
Consistent formatting and logging in one place.

questions.q: What are health checks in ASP.NET Core and why do they matter in microservices?

answer.oneLiner: Health checks are a built-in mechanism in ASP.NET Core for exposing the operational status of your application and its dependencies through HTTP endpoints. This is essential in container-based and microservices environments because orchestrators like Kubernetes use these endpoints to decide whether to send traffic to an instance or restart it. There are typically two kinds of health checks: liveness, which just checks whether the process is alive and responding, and readiness, which checks whether the app is ready to serve traffic — including whether it can reach its database and other dependencies.
keyPoints: Health check endpoints let Kubernetes probes, load balancers, and monitoring tools programmatically verify whether your app is healthy without reading logs or guessing.
Liveness checks answer the question of whether the process is running and responsive — if this fails, Kubernetes restarts the container.

Readiness checks answer the question of whether the app is ready to serve requests right now — if this fails, traffic is withheld but the container is not restarted, which is the right behavior when a dependency is temporarily unavailable.
You set up health checks by calling AddHealthChecks in service registration, adding individual checks for your database, Redis, and other dependencies, then exposing them with MapHealthChecks.
It is important to separate liveness and readiness into different endpoints because a failing database connection should pull the pod from rotation, not trigger a restart loop.

code.note: Separate liveness and readiness endpoints with tags/predicates.

redFlags: Returning healthy without checking dependencies
Confusing liveness with readiness

dialogue.text: What are health checks and why do they matter in microservices?
Health checks expose a `/health` endpoint reporting whether the app and its dependencies are working, so orchestrators and load balancers route traffic only to healthy instances.
What's the difference between liveness and readiness?

Liveness asks 'is the process alive?' — if it fails, Kubernetes restarts the pod. Readiness asks 'can it serve traffic right now?' — if it fails, traffic is withheld but the pod isn't killed, e.g. while warming up or a dependency is briefly down.
Why split the endpoints rather than one combined check?
Because the actions differ. A failing dependency shouldn't restart the pod (that won't help) — it should just pull it from rotation. Conflating them causes needless restart loops.

followUpsQA.a: Liveness = is it alive (restart if not); readiness = can it serve traffic (withhold if not).
Different failures need different actions — restart vs pull from rotation.

followUpsQA.q: What should a health check verify?
followUpsQA.a: Dependency reachability — DB, Redis, message broker.
Kubernetes and load balancers to restart or stop routing.

questions.q: How do you run background tasks with IHostedService / BackgroundService?

answer.oneLiner: BackgroundService is the base class for long-running tasks that run alongside your ASP.NET Core application — things like processing a message queue, running a scheduled job, or polling an external API. You derive from it, override the ExecuteAsync method, and register it with AddHostedService, and the host starts and stops it automatically with the application lifecycle. The main gotcha is that BackgroundService is effectively a singleton, so you cannot inject scoped services like DbContext directly into the constructor — you need to use IServiceScopeFactory to create a fresh scope each time you do a unit of work.
keyPoints: BackgroundService is a convenience base class over IHostedService that gives you a single ExecuteAsync method to implement rather than managing start and stop methods separately.

ExecuteAsync runs for the entire lifetime of the application, so you typically loop inside it — either with a periodic timer for scheduled work or by listening to a queue or channel for incoming work.

The CancellationToken passed to ExecuteAsync is signaled when the host is shutting down, and you must respect it by passing it to your async calls and checking it in your loop so the service stops cleanly.

Because BackgroundService is a singleton, injecting a scoped service like DbContext into the constructor creates a captive dependency — instead, inject IServiceScopeFactory and create a new scope for each batch of work.

code.note: Create a DI scope inside the loop to use scoped services like DbContext.

redFlags: Ignoring the CancellationToken
Injecting a scoped service into the constructor

dialogue.text: How do you run background tasks with BackgroundService?
Derive from `BackgroundService` — a convenience base over `IHostedService` — and override `ExecuteAsync` to run long-lived work like polling, queue processing, or scheduled jobs alongside the app.
BackgroundService is a singleton — how do you use a scoped DbContext in it?

I inject `IServiceScopeFactory`, then inside the work loop create a scope and resolve the DbContext from it, disposing the scope each iteration. Injecting the DbContext directly would be a captive dependency.
How does graceful shutdown work here?

The framework passes a `CancellationToken` that's signaled on shutdown. I honor it — pass it to async calls and check it in the loop — so in-flight work stops cleanly instead of being killed mid-operation.

followUpsQA.q: How do you use a scoped DbContext in a BackgroundService?
followUpsQA.a: Create a scope via `IServiceScopeFactory` inside the loop and resolve from it.
Honor the `CancellationToken` the framework signals on shutdown.

followUpsQA.q: Why not inject DbContext in the constructor?
followUpsQA.a: BackgroundService is a singleton — it'd be a captive dependency.

followUpsQA.q: How do you schedule periodic work?
followUpsQA.a: With `PeriodicTimer`, or a library like Hangfire/Quartz.

questions.q: Output caching (.NET 7+) vs response caching — what's the difference?

answer.oneLiner: Response caching and output caching both reduce server load by avoiding recomputing the same response repeatedly, but they work in fundamentally different ways. Response caching works by setting HTTP cache headers on the response and relying on clients or downstream proxies to honor them — but you have no control over whether they actually do. Output caching, introduced in .NET 7, is entirely server-side — the framework stores the response in memory and serves it directly, regardless of what the client sends, and it supports tag-based eviction so you can programmatically invalidate cached entries when data changes.
keyPoints: Response caching sets Cache-Control and related HTTP headers on the response, which instructs browsers and intermediate proxies to cache the response — but they can ignore these headers.

Output caching stores the response on the server side in a cache store you control, so caching actually happens even when clients send Cache-Control: no-cache or other headers that bypass client-side caching.
Output caching supports tag-based eviction, where you label cached responses with tags and then call EvictByTagAsync to invalidate all cached responses sharing a tag when the underlying data changes.

Response caching is still useful for leveraging CDN and proxy caches to reduce load at the network edge, while output caching is better when you need guaranteed server-side control over what is cached and when it is cleared.

code.note: Tag-based eviction is the key advantage over response caching.

redFlags: Thinking response caching guarantees caching (clients can ignore it)

dialogue.text: Output caching vs response caching — what's the difference?

Response caching is header-driven — it mostly serves downstream proxy and client caches, which can ignore the headers. Output caching, new in .NET 7, is a server-side store the app fully controls, including tag-based invalidation.
Why is server-side control better than header-only?

Because with response caching a client or proxy can just ignore the cache headers, so you can't rely on it. Output caching stores the response on the server, so caching actually happens regardless of client behavior.
How does tag-based eviction help?
You tag cached entries — say all reports with `"reports"` — and when underlying data changes you call `EvictByTagAsync("reports")` to invalidate them all at once. Response caching has no equivalent.

followUpsQA.a: Caching happens regardless of whether clients honor headers.
Tag entries, then call `EvictByTagAsync` to invalidate a group.
HTTP cache headers, which clients/proxies may ignore.
Output caching, added in .NET 7.

questions.q: Blazor Server vs Blazor WebAssembly vs SSR — brief comparison.

answer.oneLiner: Blazor is Microsoft's framework for building interactive web UIs using C# instead of JavaScript, and it comes in three different rendering models that suit different scenarios. Blazor Server runs your component code on the server and streams UI updates to the browser over a persistent SignalR connection — it starts fast because there is almost nothing to download, but it requires a constant connection and has some latency sensitivity. Blazor WebAssembly runs the .NET runtime and your C# code directly in the browser using WebAssembly, so it works offline but has a larger initial download. In .NET 8, these became render modes you can mix per component in a single application.
keyPoints: Blazor Server runs C# on the server and sends minimal UI diff updates to the browser over a SignalR WebSocket connection, so the initial page load is very fast because there is almost no client-side download.

Blazor WebAssembly downloads the .NET runtime and your compiled assemblies to the browser and runs everything client-side, which means it works offline but has a significantly larger initial download payload.

Server-Side Rendering in .NET 8 renders Blazor components to static HTML on the server for fast first paint and good SEO, with the option to add interactive components using Server or WebAssembly render modes.
In .NET 8, Blazor Unified introduced render modes as a per-component concept, so you can mix SSR pages with interactive Server or WebAssembly components in the same application.
Blazor Server needs a reliable, low-latency connection between the browser and server because every user interaction makes a round trip to the server before the UI updates.
redFlags: Saying Blazor Server runs in the browser
Not knowing WASM has a larger initial payload

dialogue.text: Blazor Server vs WebAssembly vs SSR — brief comparison.

Blazor Server runs C# on the server and pushes UI diffs over a SignalR connection. WebAssembly runs C# in the browser via WASM. SSR, in .NET 8, renders static HTML on the server with optional interactivity.
When would you pick WASM over Server?
When I need offline support or to offload work to the client, and can tolerate a larger initial download. Server needs a constant low-latency connection; WASM runs fully client-side once loaded.
What changed in .NET 8 here?
It unifies these as render modes you can mix per component — you can render a page SSR for fast first paint and make just the interactive parts Server or WASM.

followUpsQA.q: Does Blazor Server run in the browser?
followUpsQA.a: No — C# runs on the server; only UI diffs go to the browser.
A larger initial download payload.
Per-component modes (SSR, Server, WASM) you can mix in one app.

followUpsQA.q: What does Blazor Server use for transport?
followUpsQA.a: A SignalR connection.

questions.q: How do you prevent over-posting / mass assignment in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: Over-posting, also called mass assignment, is a vulnerability where a client sends extra fields in the request body that get automatically bound to properties they should not be able to set. For example, if you bind a request directly to your entity class and the entity has an IsAdmin property, a malicious client can include IsAdmin: true in their JSON and it gets applied. The fix is to always bind requests to purpose-built input DTOs that expose only the fields the client is permitted to change, and then manually map those values onto your entity on the server side.
keyPoints: Over-posting happens when model binding maps a property from the request body to a field on your entity that the client should never be able to set, like IsAdmin, Price, or Role.
The fix is to define dedicated request DTO classes that contain only the fields a client is allowed to provide, and bind the incoming request to those DTOs instead of your entity classes.
After binding to the DTO, you explicitly copy the allowed values onto the entity on the server side, which gives you full control over exactly what can be changed.
The Bind attribute can whitelist specific properties for binding, but a purpose-built DTO is clearer and less error-prone because the type itself communicates exactly what the endpoint accepts.

code.note: The DTO defines exactly what the client is permitted to change.

redFlags: Binding requests straight to EF entities
Trusting all incoming fields

dialogue.text: How do you prevent over-posting / mass assignment?
Bind requests to purpose-built input DTOs that contain only the fields a client may set — never directly to your EF entities — so an attacker can't smuggle in fields like `IsAdmin` or `Price`.
What's the concrete attack if you bind straight to the entity?
The client posts extra JSON fields that happen to match entity properties — `"IsAdmin": true` or `"Price": 0` — and the binder sets them. You then save and you've granted admin or zeroed the price.
Isn't the [Bind] allow-list enough?
`[Bind]` works, but it's easy to forget and lives on the action. A dedicated DTO is explicit and self-documenting — the type itself defines exactly what the client can change.

followUpsQA.q: Why not bind directly to EF entities?
followUpsQA.a: Clients could set fields they shouldn't, like `IsAdmin` or `Price`.
An allow-list of bindable properties — works but DTOs are cleaner.
Bind to a request DTO, then map allowed fields to the entity server-side.

followUpsQA.q: How does model binding pick fields?
followUpsQA.a: It maps matching request fields onto the target type's properties.

questions.q: Sync vs async controller actions — when does it actually matter?

answer.oneLiner: Making a controller action async versus synchronous is not about making that individual request run faster — the network round trip and database query take the same amount of time either way. The difference is about scalability under load. When you use synchronous blocking I/O, the thread handling the request just sits there doing nothing while it waits for the database to respond, wasting a thread-pool thread. With async, the thread is returned to the pool during the wait and can handle other requests, which means the server can serve far more concurrent requests before running out of threads.
keyPoints: An async action does not make a single request faster — the I/O operation takes the same amount of time — but it allows the thread to do other work while waiting, which improves throughput.
A synchronous action blocks a thread-pool thread for the entire duration of the request including I/O waits, which wastes the thread and limits how many concurrent requests the server can handle.
Thread-pool starvation occurs when all threads are blocked on synchronous I/O and new requests cannot get a thread, causing them to queue up and response times to spike dramatically.

For CPU-bound work where the thread is actually computing rather than waiting for I/O, async does not help — the thread is busy either way, and you would use parallelism instead to distribute the work.

code.note: Async all the way; never block on async with .Result/.Wait().

redFlags: Thinking async makes a single request faster
Calling .Result in an action

dialogue.text: Sync vs async controller actions — when does it actually matter?

For I/O-bound work — DB, HTTP, files — async frees the thread while waiting, so you serve far more concurrent requests. Synchronous blocking holds the thread doing nothing and causes thread-pool starvation under load.
Does async make a single request faster?
No — a single request isn't any faster; the I/O takes the same time. Async improves throughput and scalability, not per-request latency. That's the common misconception.
What about CPU-bound work — should that be async?
Async gains nothing for CPU-bound work — there's no I/O wait to release the thread during. Async is specifically for freeing the thread while awaiting I/O.

followUpsQA.a: All pool threads are blocked, so new requests queue and latency spikes.

followUpsQA.q: Does async speed up CPU-bound work?
followUpsQA.a: No — async only helps I/O-bound waits.

followUpsQA.q: Does async make one request faster?
followUpsQA.a: No — it improves throughput/scalability, not single-request latency.

followUpsQA.q: What must you never do in an action?
followUpsQA.a: Block on async with `.Result`/`.Wait()`.

questions.q: How does logging work in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: ASP.NET Core has a built-in logging abstraction — ILogger<T> — that you inject anywhere and call methods like LogInformation, LogWarning, LogError. Providers (Console, EventLog, Serilog, Application Insights) receive the log entries. Log levels filter which entries are written; you configure them per namespace in appsettings.json.
keyPoints: ILogger<T> is the abstraction you inject. T is the category name (usually the class name) that appears in the output so you know where a log entry came from.
Log levels from lowest to highest: Trace, Debug, Information, Warning, Error, Critical, None. You set a minimum level per provider and per category in appsettings.json.

Providers: the built-in ones are Console, Debug, EventSource, EventLog (Windows). Third-party ones like Serilog and NLog integrate via AddSerilog() and support sinks (files, Seq, Application Insights, Elastic).
Structured logging with message templates preserves properties as searchable fields: LogInformation("Order {OrderId} placed by {UserId}", id, userId) — Serilog and Seq make this queryable.
Always use message templates with placeholders, never string interpolation — interpolation bakes the value in and loses structure.

code.note: Use placeholders {Name}, not $"...", to keep structured logging queryable

redFlags: Using string interpolation in log calls instead of message templates.
Not knowing ILogger<T> is injected from DI, not instantiated directly.

dialogue.text: How do you add logging to an ASP.NET Core service?

Inject ILogger<T> via the constructor — T becomes the category name. Call LogInformation, LogWarning, LogError with message templates. Configure minimum levels per namespace in appsettings.json and add providers like Serilog for file or cloud sinks.
Why use message templates instead of string interpolation?
Templates keep the structured data separate so tools like Seq or Application Insights can index and query on OrderId as a field. Interpolation collapses it to a string and you lose that ability.

followUpsQA.a: Serilog adds structured log sinks (file, Seq, Elastic, Azure), enrichers, and filtering — the built-in providers only cover console/EventLog.
A context block (using (_log.BeginScope(...))) that attaches properties to all log entries inside it — useful for correlating request logs with a TraceId.

questions.q: How can multiple controllers share the same view, or a controller use multiple views in ASP.NET Core MVC?

answer.oneLiner: You share a view by placing it in the /Views/Shared/ folder — any controller can return it by name. You return a different view from a controller action by passing the view name to View(): return View("AnotherView", model). This is how you reuse layouts, partial views, and common templates.
keyPoints: Views/Shared/: files here are accessible by all controllers. Layouts (_Layout.cshtml), error pages, and commonly shared partial views live here.
return View(): by default renders the view matching the action name. return View("ViewName") renders a specific view by name regardless of the action.
return View("~/Views/OtherController/SomeView.cshtml") renders a view from another controller's folder using the tilde-rooted path.
Partial views: a view fragment (e.g., _ProductCard.cshtml in Shared/) that any view can include with @Html.PartialAsync or <partial name="...">.
View Components are the modern alternative to partial views when the fragment needs its own logic (e.g., a shopping cart count).

code.note: Shared/ views are resolved before controller-specific folders in the view engine search order

redFlags: Not knowing about the Shared/ folder lookup order.
Duplicating the same .cshtml file across controller folders instead of moving it to Shared/.

dialogue.text: How do two controllers share the same view?

Put it in Views/Shared/. The MVC view engine always looks in Shared/ as a fallback, so any controller can return View("ViewName") and find it there. If the view is only shared by a couple of controllers and has specific logic, I'd consider a View Component instead.

followUpsQA.a: 1. Views/[ControllerName]/[ActionName].cshtml → 2. Views/Shared/[ActionName].cshtml.
Partial views are pure HTML fragments. View Components have their own C# logic and DI — use them when the fragment needs to query data or call a service.

questions.q: How do you handle dropdown performance in an ASP.NET Core MVC application?

answer.oneLiner: The most common dropdown performance problem is re-querying the database on every page load for data that rarely changes — countries, categories, roles. The fix is caching: cache the SelectList in IMemoryCache (or IDistributedCache for multi-server), populate it once, and refresh only when the data changes.
keyPoints: Static or near-static dropdown data (countries, status codes) should be cached in IMemoryCache with a sliding or absolute expiry so the database is only hit once per cache miss.
Use ViewBag/ViewData sparingly for passing a SelectList to the view; prefer a strongly-typed ViewModel that includes a property of type IEnumerable<SelectListItem>.
For very large datasets, consider cascading dropdowns with AJAX — load only the top-level list on page load and fetch sub-options by API call when the user selects a parent item.
If the data changes infrequently, seed it at application startup and hold it in a singleton service instead of hitting the database at all.
For high-traffic pages: combine caching with Tag Helper output caching or Response Caching so the entire partial view is cached.

code.note: Invalidate the cache key when a category is created/updated/deleted

redFlags: Querying the database for static dropdown data on every page load.
Loading 10,000 options into a single dropdown — use typeahead/search instead.

dialogue.text: We have a dropdown of categories that appears on every page. It's slow — how do you fix it?

Cache it. Categories rarely change so I'd use IMemoryCache.GetOrCreateAsync with a 1-hour expiry and invalidate the key whenever a category is saved. That way the database is only hit on the first load and after changes. For very large lists I'd switch to an AJAX-powered typeahead instead of loading everything upfront.

followUpsQA.a: MemoryCache lives in the process — fast but not shared across web farm nodes. DistributedCache (Redis) is shared across all instances.

followUpsQA.q: When would you skip caching and use a static list?
followUpsQA.a: If the data is fixed at deployment time (e.g., status codes) — hard-code it as a static list, no database needed.

questions.q: Where does an injected dependency actually reside in memory?

answer.oneLiner: All injected services are reference types and live on the managed heap. Their lifetime — how long the GC keeps them alive — is controlled by the DI container: Singleton services are held by the root IServiceProvider for the application's lifetime; Scoped services are held by the per-request IServiceScope; Transient services are released as soon as the scope that resolved them is disposed.
keyPoints: Services are objects on the heap, pointed to by the IServiceProvider's internal dictionary. As long as the container holds a reference, the GC won't collect them.
Singleton: one instance per application. The root container holds a strong reference to it for the process lifetime — it's effectively a static object.

Scoped: one instance per request (or per scope). The per-request IServiceScope holds the reference; when the request ends and the scope is disposed, the reference is released and the GC can collect it.
Transient: new instance every time it's resolved. The scope holds no long-lived reference — the object can be collected as soon as the dependent that received it goes out of scope.
Captive dependency problem: if a Singleton holds a Scoped service, the Singleton keeps the Scoped service alive for the application's lifetime — longer than intended, causing bugs.
redFlags: Injecting a Scoped service into a Singleton (captive dependency bug).
Thinking transient services are collected immediately — they live as long as their dependent.

dialogue.text: Where in memory does an injected service actually live?

On the managed heap, held alive by the container's reference. A Singleton is held by the root IServiceProvider for the app's lifetime. A Scoped service is held by the per-request scope and collected after the request ends. Transient has no long-lived container reference — it lives as long as the object that received it.
What is the captive dependency problem?

Injecting a Scoped service into a Singleton. The Singleton keeps a reference to the Scoped service for the whole app lifetime, bypassing its intended per-request scope — the ASP.NET Core runtime throws on this in development by default.

followUpsQA.q: How does ASP.NET Core detect captive dependencies?
followUpsQA.a: The DI container's ValidateScopes option (on by default in Development) throws on startup if a shorter-lived service is captured by a longer-lived one.

questions.q: What is the difference between HTTP 200 OK and 201 Created?

answer.oneLiner: 200 OK means the request succeeded and the response body contains the result. 201 Created means a new resource was created as a result of the request, and the Location header points to the URL of the newly created resource. In REST, POST to a collection should return 201, not 200.
keyPoints: 200 OK: general success. Used for GET (here's the data), PUT (updated, here's the resource), and DELETE (deleted) responses.
201 Created: the server created a new resource. The Location header should contain the URI of the new resource (e.g., /api/orders/42). Often includes the new resource in the body.
In ASP.NET Core: return Ok(data) → 200; return CreatedAtAction(nameof(GetById), new { id }, data) → 201 with a correct Location header built automatically.
Why it matters: API clients can rely on 201 to know where to find the newly created item without parsing the body.

code.note: CreatedAtAction builds the Location header automatically using the route

redFlags: Returning 200 from a POST that creates a resource — semantically wrong.
Returning 201 without a Location header — clients need it to find the new resource.

dialogue.text: When should a POST return 201 instead of 200?

Always when a new resource is created. 201 signals creation and includes a Location header pointing to the new resource's URL. I use CreatedAtAction in ASP.NET Core which builds the header automatically.

followUpsQA.q: What should the Location header contain?
followUpsQA.a: The URL of the newly created resource, e.g., https://api.example.com/orders/42.

followUpsQA.q: What status for a PUT that creates if not exists (upsert)?
followUpsQA.a: 200 if it updated, 201 if it created — or simply 200 for simplicity in most APIs.

questions.q: Can you use the [HttpPost] attribute on a GET action in ASP.NET Core? What happens?

answer.oneLiner: Yes, you can put [HttpPost] on any action method regardless of what you name it. The attribute controls which HTTP verb the route responds to, not the method name. But you should not — naming a method Get* while it only responds to POST is a deliberate lie in your code that will confuse every developer and client who reads it.
keyPoints: [HttpGet], [HttpPost], [HttpPut], etc., are route attributes that constrain the verb a route will match — they are independent of the C# method name.
If you put [HttpPost] on a method named GetOrders(), ASP.NET Core happily routes POST /orders to it. It compiles and runs without error.
The practical result is that a GET /orders request will return 405 Method Not Allowed, which will confuse clients who expect a GET endpoint named GetOrders.
REST conventions and readable code both demand that your verb attribute and method name agree — a semantic mismatch is a maintenance hazard.

code.note: Multiple verb attributes on one action is valid for HEAD/GET pairs

redFlags: Thinking [HttpPost] on a Get method is a compile error — it isn't.
Mixing verb and method name conventions without understanding the routing implications.

dialogue.text: What happens if you put [HttpPost] on a method called GetOrders?

It compiles and works — the action will only respond to POST requests. GET requests to that route will get a 405. The verb attribute is what controls routing, not the method name. But it's semantically wrong and a code smell.

followUpsQA.q: Can one action respond to multiple verbs?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — stack multiple attributes: [HttpGet][HttpHead] for a HEAD-compatible endpoint.
